title: Generic shapes (rectangle/diamond/ellipse) ignore currentItemRoundness — always created sharp
evidence: Our generic branch calls newElement({type, x, y, width:0, height:0, ...this.#createStyle()}). #createStyle() (lines 603-623) returns only strokeColor/backgroundColor/fillStyle/strokeWidth/strokeStyle/roughness/opacity — no roundness. newElement's _newElementBase defaults roundness to null. Excalidraw passes roundness: this.getCurrentItemRoundness(elementType), which returns {type: ADAPTIVE_RADIUS|PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS} when currentItemRoundness === 'round'.
expected: With the default appState (getDefaultAppState sets currentItemRoundness:'round'), rectangles/diamonds/ellipses are created with rounded corners (roundness object set).
actual: Every generic shape is created with roundness:null (sharp corners) regardless of the currentItemRoundness setting; the round/sharp toggle has no effect on newly drawn shapes.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed genuine, user-visible divergence.

PORT (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts): The generic-shape creation branch (the `else` at line 2362, the catch-all after freedraw/arrow/line/frame/embeddable, which handles rectangle/diamond/ellipse) calls `newElement({type, x, y, width:0, height:0, ...this.#createStyle()})`. `#createStyle()` (lines 603-623) returns ONLY strokeColor/backgroundColor/fillStyle/strokeWidth/strokeStyle/roughness/opacity — no roundness. `_newElementBase` in src/lib/element/newElement.ts defaults `roundness = null` (line 96, applied at line 144). So every generic shape is created with roundness:null (sharp), regardless of currentItemRoundness. The round/sharp toggle (`setEdges`, lines 551-565) only mutates roundness on already-SELECTED elements; it never feeds creation.

EXCALIDRAW (App.tsx): `createGenericElementOnPointerDown` (9510) sets `roundness: this.getCurrentItemRoundness(elementType)` (9537). `getCurrentItemRoundness` (9492-9508) returns `{type: ADAPTIVE_RADIUS|PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS}` when `state.currentItemRoundness === 'round'`, else null. `isUsingAdaptiveRadius` (typeChecks.ts:308) includes rectangle; diamond falls to PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS.

DEFAULTS: Both src/lib/excalidraw/appState.ts:39 and excalidraw-master appState.ts:39 set `currentItemRoundness: isTestEnv() ? 'sharp' : 'round'`. So in a real (non-test) session the default is 'round'.

RENDER IMPACT: The renderer branches on `element.roundness` (renderElement.ts:449 `if (element.roundness && context.roundRect)`, shape.ts via getCornerRadius, bounds.ts:922/1077/1104). null vs object yields sharp vs rounded corners — visibly different.

NET: Open the app (default round) and draw a rectangle/diamond → excalidraw renders rounded corners; the port renders sharp. The round/sharp toggle has no effect on newly drawn shapes in the port. Real behavioral bug.

Minor nuance (does not refute): ellipse is in neither adaptive nor proportional list in excalidraw, so it would get `{type: undefined}` — still a non-null object vs the port's null, and ellipse roundness handling differs anyway; the rectangle/diamond cases are unambiguously divergent.

title: Arrow creation ignores currentItemArrowType 'round' — arrows never get proportional roundness
evidence: Our arrow branch passes only elbowed + #createStyle(); no roundness. Excalidraw: roundness: this.state.currentItemArrowType === ARROW_TYPE.round ? {type: ROUNDNESS.PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS} : null. Default appState currentItemArrowType = ARROW_TYPE.round (appState.ts:40).
expected: By default arrows are created with roundness:{PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS} so multi-bend/curved arrows render rounded.
actual: Arrows are created with roundness:null always; the round arrow type produces sharp corners.

verdict.reasoning: Verified both cited locations and the rendering path. Upstream App.tsx:9350-9355 creates arrows with `roundness: this.state.currentItemArrowType === ARROW_TYPE.round ? {type: ROUNDNESS.PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS} : null`, and the default `currentItemArrowType` is `ARROW_TYPE.round` in both upstream (excalidraw-master/packages/excalidraw/appState.ts:40) and our port (src/lib/excalidraw/appState.ts:40). Our arrow branch (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts:2334-2346) passes only `elbowed` and `...this.#createStyle()`. `#createStyle()` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:604-623) returns no `roundness` field, and `newArrowElement` (src/lib/element/newElement.ts:487-525) never sets roundness, so `_newElementBase` defaults it to `roundness = null`. Result: our arrows are always created with `roundness: null`, regardless of the round/sharp toggle. This is behaviorally observable: in shape generation (src/lib/element/shape.ts:910-924), `!element.roundness` produces `generator.linearPath` (sharp straight segments) while a truthy roundness produces `generator.curve` (smooth Catmull-Rom curve). Multi-point linear/arrow editing is supported in our port (draw-controller lines 262, 1970). So a user drawing a multi-bend arrow with default settings gets a smooth curved arrow in real Excalidraw but a sharp-cornered polyline in our port, and the "round" arrow-type button has no effect on newly created arrows. A plain 2-point straight arrow looks identical either way, which is why this only manifests on multi-segment arrows — but that is a realistic user action, so the divergence is genuine. Severity is below 'bug' full because the most common single-segment arrow is unaffected and there is no crash or data loss; it is a visible rendering/feature divergence on a subset of arrows, i.e. partial.

title: Line creation ignores currentItemRoundness — lines never rounded
evidence: Our line branch passes only #createStyle(); no roundness. Excalidraw: roundness: this.state.currentItemRoundness === 'round' ? {type: ROUNDNESS.PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS} : null.
expected: Lines drawn with currentItemRoundness 'round' (the default) get roundness:{PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS}.
actual: Lines are always roundness:null (sharp); the round/sharp control has no effect on lines.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed genuine divergence. CITED CODE MATCHES CLAIM: Our line-creation branch (draw-controller.svelte.ts:2347-2355) builds the element via newLinearElement({ type:"line", x, y, points:[...], ...this.#createStyle() }). #createStyle() (lines 604-622) returns ONLY strokeColor/backgroundColor/fillStyle/strokeWidth/strokeStyle/roughness/opacity — no roundness. newLinearElement (newElement.ts:458-485) does not set roundness either, and _newElementBase defaults `roundness = null` (newElement.ts:96). So a newly drawn line always gets roundness:null.

Excalidraw (App.tsx:9366-9383) sets roundness on the line: this.state.currentItemRoundness === "round" ? { type: ROUNDNESS.PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS } : null. Exactly as cited.

DEFAULT IS 'round' IN BOTH: our appState.ts:39 sets currentItemRoundness: isTestEnv() ? "sharp" : "round" — so in real (non-test) use the default is "round", matching Excalidraw. There is a real UI control: the controller's get edges()/setEdges() (lines 548-562) toggles currentItemRoundness and applies roundness to selected elements, so the round/sharp control exists and is meaningful.

VISIBLE EFFECT CONFIRMED: our shape generator (shape.ts:601-629) for type 'line': if !element.roundness it emits straight move/lineTo ops (sharp); if roundness is truthy it emits generator.curve → bcurveTo (smooth curve). So roundness genuinely changes rendering. Multi-point lines are supported (controller hint line 1749 'Click to start multiple points'). Therefore: a user drawing a multi-point line with the default 'round' edge setting gets a smooth curved line in Excalidraw but straight/sharp segments in our port; toggling the round/sharp control has no effect on the line's curvature at creation time. (A 2-point line is visually identical either way since curve over 2 points is a straight segment, but 3+ point lines diverge clearly.)

This is not collab/AI/i18n scope; it is core tools-creation behavior. The divergence is real and user-visible.

title: Single-click with line/arrow tool discards element instead of entering multi-point mode
evidence: Our pointerUp discards any generic|linear element with width<1 && height<1 (the 'discarded' branch). The controller has no multiElement state — only isLineEditing for editing existing linear elements (lines 1970-1975). Excalidraw, on a non-drag linear pointerUp, sets {multiElement: newElement, newElement} and mutates points so subsequent clicks add points, then finalizes on loop/threshold/double-click.
expected: Clicking (without dragging) with the line or arrow tool starts a multi-point polyline/arrow: each subsequent click adds a vertex; Enter / clicking near the start / double-click finalizes.
actual: A click without a drag deletes the linear element entirely. Multi-point line/arrow creation is impossible — only single drag-segments can be drawn.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against both cited locations and the surrounding code.

OURS (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts): pointerDown for the `line`/`arrow` tools always creates a fresh 2-point linear element (lines 2334-2355) — there is no branch that checks for an in-progress multi-point element to append a vertex to. On pointerUp, lines 2723-2732 unconditionally discard any `generic`/`linear` element whose width<1 && height<1 (i.e., a click with no drag): it filters the element out of `#elements` and replaces the scene. The controller never reads or writes `appState.multiElement` — grep over src/lib/x/ for `multiElement` returns zero hits in the controller. The only "multi-point" support is `isLineEditing` (lines 1970-1975), which edits EXISTING linear elements via `selectedLinearElement`, not creation. Notably, the vendored AppState DOES carry a `multiElement` field (src/lib/excalidraw/types.ts:301 — documented "for multi-point linear element that's created by clicking as opposed to dragging"), initialized to null (appState.ts:80) and read by the renderer (interactiveScene.ts:1764), but nothing in the active controller ever sets it, so it stays null forever.

THEIRS (excalidraw-master App.tsx:10865-10925): on a linear-element pointerUp where the drag did not occur or `dragDistance < MINIMUM_ARROW_SIZE`, and `!multiElement`, Excalidraw mutates the element to keep its points and calls `setState({ multiElement: newElement, newElement })` — entering multi-point mode so subsequent clicks add vertices (finalized via actionFinalize on loop/threshold/double-click/Enter). It explicitly does NOT discard the element on a non-drag click.

User-visible impact: with the line or arrow tool, a single click (no drag) in the port deletes the nascent element instead of starting a click-to-add-points polyline. Multi-point lines/arrows cannot be created at all — only single drag-segments. This is aggravated by the hint bar (line 1749) which literally tells the user "Click to start multiple points, drag for single line", advertising a behavior the controller doesn't implement. This is a genuine behavioral divergence on a realistic, common user action.

title: Element creation does not snap origin to grid (gridModeEnabled / Ctrl override ignored)
evidence: Our pointerDown sets #originX/#originY = raw scene x,y. Every Excalidraw creation handler computes [gridX, gridY] = getGridPoint(origin.x, origin.y, ctrlOrCmd ? null : this.getEffectiveGridSize()) and creates the element at the snapped point. getEffectiveGridSize exists in our #linearApp() surface (line 1986) but is never used at creation time.
expected: With grid mode on, new rectangle/diamond/ellipse/line/arrow/freedraw/frame origins snap to the nearest grid point (unless Ctrl/Cmd held).
actual: New element origins are placed at the exact unsnapped cursor position; the grid never affects element creation.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed at both cited locations. Excalidraw's creation handlers all snap the origin: createGenericElementOnPointerDown (App.tsx:9514-9520), the linear-element branch (9315-9319), and createFrameElementOnPointerDown (9572-9578) each compute `const [gridX, gridY] = getGridPoint(pointerDownState.origin.x, origin.y, event[KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD] ? null : this.getEffectiveGridSize())` and build the element at the snapped point. Our port (draw-controller.svelte.ts:2319-2321) sets `this.#originX = x; this.#originY = y;` from the raw unsnapped scene coordinates and passes those straight into newElement/newArrowElement/newLinearElement/newFrameElement/newEmbeddableElement. Our `getEffectiveGridSize` does exist (line 1986-1987, returning gridSize when gridModeEnabled else null) but it is ONLY exposed inside `#linearApp()` and consumed by the linear-element editor surface — never at element creation. This is a genuine, user-observable divergence: grid mode is a real toggleable feature in our port (toggleGrid at line 417, command palette / ⌘' shortcut, and the grid is actually drawn on canvas via strokeGrid in staticScene.ts:264-274 when gridModeEnabled). So a user turns grid mode on, sees the grid, draws a rectangle, and in excalidraw the origin snaps to the nearest grid point while in our port it lands at the exact unsnapped cursor position. The Ctrl/Cmd override to disable snapping is also entirely absent. Two minor inaccuracies in the claim, neither of which negates the divergence: (1) freedraw is NOT snapped in excalidraw either — handleFreeDrawElementOnPointerDown (9015-9019) passes `null` as the grid size unconditionally, so listing freedraw among snapped types is wrong; (2) the divergence is real for generic shapes (rectangle/diamond/ellipse), line, arrow, embeddable, and frame. Severity is a functional bug rather than cosmetic because the resulting element coordinates genuinely differ (origin and therefore all derived geometry), affecting snapping-dependent layouts and the determinism a grid is meant to provide. Note also the stale comment at line 415 ("snapping-to-grid follows gridModeEnabled") which does not match the code — no snapping occurs.

title: New elements are never parented to the frame under the cursor (frameId always null)
evidence: None of our creation branches compute getTopLayerFrameAtSceneCoords or pass frameId; newElement/newFreeDrawElement/newArrowElement/newLinearElement default frameId:null. Excalidraw computes topLayerFrame and passes frameId: topLayerFrame ? topLayerFrame.id : null for generic, freedraw, and linear elements.
expected: Drawing a shape/freedraw/arrow inside an existing frame assigns that element to the frame (frameId set), so it clips to and moves with the frame.
actual: Elements drawn inside a frame are not adopted by it at creation (frameId stays null); only frame-creation-time adoption via getElementsInNewFrame exists.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified at both cited locations. Excalidraw assigns frameId at element creation in all three relevant branches: createGenericElementOnPointerDown (App.tsx:9521-9525 computes `topLayerFrame = this.getTopLayerFrameAtSceneCoords(...)` and 9539 passes `frameId: topLayerFrame ? topLayerFrame.id : null` into the baseElementAttributes used by newElement/newEmbeddableElement); the freedraw branch (9021-9024 computes topLayerFrame, 9042 passes `frameId: topLayerFrame ? topLayerFrame.id : null` to newFreeDrawElement); and the linear branch (9321-9324 computes topLayerFrame, 9359 and 9382 pass `frameId: topLayerFrame ? topLayerFrame.id : null` to newArrowElement/newLinearElement). getTopLayerFrameAtSceneCoords (App.tsx:6733) unconditionally returns the topmost unlocked frame under the cursor — no flag gating.

Our port's pointerDown (draw-controller.svelte.ts:2322-2372) constructs newFreeDrawElement, newArrowElement, newLinearElement, newEmbeddableElement, and newElement with no frameId argument and never computes a top-layer frame. grep confirms src/lib/x/ never references `frameId` at all, and newElement.ts:94 defaults `frameId = null`. The only frame-adoption path in the controller is the inverse case at pointerUp (2736-2745): when the freshly-drawn element *is itself a frame*, it adopts elements already enclosed (getElementsInNewFrame/addElementsToFrame). There is no path where a shape/freedraw/arrow drawn inside a pre-existing frame is adopted by it.

Frames are a fully working feature in the port: src/lib/element/frame.ts implements getFrameChildren, clipping, and duplicate frameId remapping keyed off element.frameId, and the renderer clips children by frameId. Therefore the runtime divergence is real and user-visible: in Excalidraw, drawing a rectangle/arrow/freedraw inside an existing frame makes it a child (clips to the frame, moves with it); in the port the element stays frameId:null, so it does not clip to or move with the frame. This is a genuine behavioral bug, not benign/out-of-scope.

title: Freedraw never captures pen pressure; simulatePressure hardcoded true
evidence: Our freedraw is created with simulatePressure:true, pressures:[] unconditionally, and pointerMove only pushes points (never a pressure value). PointerMods (lines 181-190) has no pressure/pointerType field, so the controller cannot read event.pressure. Excalidraw: simulatePressure = event.pressure === 0.5 (mouse) ; on each move/up pushes [...pressures, event.pressure] when not simulating.
expected: With a pen, simulatePressure is false and the real per-point pressure array drives variable stroke width.
actual: All freedraw strokes use simulated pressure with an empty pressures array; pen pressure is discarded, so pen strokes look identical to mouse strokes.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed accurate in every cited particular by reading all code paths.

PORT (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts):
- Line 2323-2332: freedraw element created with `simulatePressure: true` and `pressures: []` hardcoded, unconditionally.
- Line 2534-2542 (pointerMove freedraw branch): only `mutateElement(el, map, { points: [...el.points, pointFrom(dx, dy)] })` — pushes a point, never a pressure value, never touches `el.pressures`.
- PointerMods type (lines 181-190) has shiftKey/altKey/ctrlKey/metaKey/spaceKey/button only — no `pressure` or `pointerType` field. So the controller structurally cannot receive pressure.
- EditorPreview.svelte onpointerdown/onpointermove (lines 122-172) have the real `PointerEvent` (which carries `.pressure`) but only forward modifier keys; `e.pressure` is never read or plumbed.

EXCALIDRAW (App.tsx):
- Line ~9026: `const simulatePressure = event.pressure === 0.5;` (mouse/trackpad report exactly 0.5; pens report real pressure).
- Line ~9044: `pressures: simulatePressure ? [] : [event.pressure]`.
- Lines 10377-10385 (pointerMove): `const pressures = newElement.simulatePressure ? newElement.pressures : [...newElement.pressures, event.pressure]` then mutates with both points and pressures.
- Lines 10851-10857 (pointerUp): same conditional `[...newElement.pressures, childEvent.pressure]`.

RENDER PATH (src/lib/element/shape.ts:1181-1199) confirms the consequence is visible: getFreedrawOutlinePoints feeds `element.points` (no pressure) to perfect-freehand with `simulatePressure: true` when simulatePressure is set; only when false does it map per-point `element.pressures[i]` into the [x,y,pressure] tuples. So real pen pressure would drive variable-width strokes — the machinery exists and is wired — but the controller never supplies real pressure, so it's dead for pen input.

This is a genuine runtime divergence: a pen/stylus user (variable event.pressure) gets velocity-simulated strokes identical to a mouse, whereas Excalidraw gives pressure-driven variable width. Not collab/AI/i18n out-of-scope; it's core freedraw tool behavior.

Severity is "partial" rather than "bug": for the dominant desktop input (mouse/trackpad, pressure always 0.5), Excalidraw itself sets simulatePressure=true, so the port and Excalidraw are byte-identical for that case. The divergence only manifests with a true pressure-sensitive device, where the freedraw tool works but loses a fidelity feature (pen-pressure-modulated stroke width). The tool functions; one input modality is degraded.

title: Text tool always creates a free-floating empty text; no container binding, no edit-existing
evidence: Our text branch: #select(null); newTextElement({text:'', x, y}); push; select; set editingTextId. No getElementAtPosition / getTextBindableContainerAtPosition / hasBoundTextElement handling. Excalidraw resolves the container under the click, recenters on a bound-text element, and calls startTextEditing with the container + insertAtParentCenter:!altKey, editing existing bound text instead of stacking a new element.
expected: Clicking the text tool on a rectangle (or other bindable container) inserts text bound and centered inside it; clicking on existing text edits that text.
actual: It always drops a new unbound empty text element at the raw click point, overlapping any container/text already there — text is never bound to a shape.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading both cited locations. Our text-tool branch (draw-controller.svelte.ts:2288-2304) does exactly what the claim says: on pointer-down with activeTool==="text" it calls #select(null), unconditionally constructs a new empty newTextElement({text:"", x, y}) at the raw scene click point, pushes it, selects it, and sets editingTextId. It never resolves what is under the cursor. Grep confirms there is NO getElementAtPosition/getTextBindableContainerAtPosition/hasBoundTextElement/startTextEditing/insertAtParentCenter logic anywhere in draw-controller.svelte.ts, and no containerId/boundElements handling in src/lib/x at all. The double-click handler (doubleClickAt, lines 2008-2019) only branches on image-crop and enterLineEditor — it never creates or edits text either (and the hint string at line 1755 even promises double-click-to-add-text, which is also unimplemented). By contrast Excalidraw's handleTextOnPointerDown (App.tsx:8965-9007) early-returns if already editing, then calls getElementAtPosition({includeBoundTextElement:true}) and getTextBindableContainerAtPosition; if the hit element hasBoundTextElement it sets container = element and recenters sceneX/sceneY to the element center; then calls startTextEditing with the resolved container and insertAtParentCenter:!event.altKey. This supports (a) clicking the text tool on a bindable container (rectangle/diamond/ellipse) to insert bound, centered text and (b) clicking on existing/bound text to edit it. The type system here already has boundElements/containerId (used by binding.ts for arrows), so container-bound text is in-scope, not an intentionally excluded collab/AI/i18n feature. Two realistic user actions diverge: clicking text-tool on a rectangle yields unbound floating text in ours vs centered bound text in Excalidraw; clicking text-tool on existing text stacks a new empty element in ours vs editing the existing text in Excalidraw. Genuine user-visible behavioral divergence.

title: Tool always reverts to selection after drawing; activeTool.locked (tool pin) not honored
evidence: Our pointerUp ends with an unconditional this.activeTool = 'selection'. There is no active-tool-lock state in the controller (grep for tool lock finds only element .locked). Excalidraw only resets to preferredSelectionTool when !activeTool.locked; when the toolbar lock/pin is on it keeps the drawing tool active for repeated creation.
expected: With the tool-lock toggle on, the active drawing tool stays selected after creating an element so the user can draw multiple shapes.
actual: The tool always switches back to selection after one element; there is no way to keep a tool active for repeated drawing.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified real on the actual Excalidraw-port surface. The cited port file src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts:203 declares activeTool = $state<Tool>("rectangle") where Tool (lines 163-175) is a bare string union with NO `locked` field. In pointerUp, after creating any element the controller unconditionally sets this.activeTool = "selection" (line 2719 for embeddable, line 2752 for the generic/linear/frame path). A repo-wide grep for tool-lock state (lockedTool/toolLock/activeTool.locked/keepTool) in src/lib/x/ returns nothing — the only "Lock" in the port is element-level (controller.lockSelected() / lockSelected context-menu and el.locked). The /x route (src/routes/x/+page.svelte → EditorPreview.svelte → new DrawController()) is a live surface, and its keydown handler (EditorPreview.svelte lines 317-413) has no Q case and no tool-lock toggle in its toolbar. Tellingly, the port's own HelpDialog.svelte:74 advertises "Keep selected tool active / Q" yet the controller never implements it.

Excalidraw's cited code confirms the intended behavior: App.tsx:10933 `if (!activeTool.locked)` gates the reset to preferredSelectionTool (so when locked it keeps the drawing tool and only nulls newElement), and App.tsx:9459 `!this.state.activeTool.locked` similarly gates selection on creation. So with the toolbar 🔒 on, Excalidraw keeps the active drawing tool for repeated creation; the port cannot — there is no way to keep a creation tool active for repeated drawing.

Refutation considered and rejected: there IS a working toolLocked implementation in src/lib/canvas/editor.svelte.ts (toolLocked $state, toggleToolLock, Q binding in routes/+page.svelte, Toolbar lock button) — but that is the legacy LayoutForge editor on the `/` route (+page.svelte imports from $lib/canvas/editor.svelte.js), a distinct pre-port codebase, not the Excalidraw port (DrawController) the claim targets and not the surface being compared against excalidraw-master. The divergence on the port surface is genuine and user-visible: a user who turns on tool-lock (or expects the advertised Q shortcut) on the /x port gets the tool reverting to selection after every single shape.

title: Eraser deletes the single topmost element at each sampled point; fast drags skip elements and there is no trail-based hit test
evidence: Our #eraseAt does a single point hitTest at the current sample and immediately filters that element out; between move samples no interpolation/segment test is done, and only one element per sample is removed. Excalidraw drives an eraserTrail that hit-tests element strokes against the continuous trail segments (and marks-then-deletes), so it reliably erases everything the path crosses.
expected: Dragging the eraser quickly across multiple elements erases every element the stroke path intersects.
actual: Only elements that happen to land exactly under a discrete pointer sample get erased; fast drags leave gaps and overlapping elements under one sample aren't all removed.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading both cited locations. Our port (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts): on pointer-down (lines 2306-2311) and every pointer-move while #erasing (lines 2418-2423), it calls #eraseAt(x,y). #eraseAt (1212-1223) does a single discrete-point #hitTest (1785-1801), which walks z-order top-down and RETURNS THE FIRST (topmost) hit element only, then immediately filters that one element out and commits. There is no interpolation between move samples and no accumulation — exactly one topmost element removed per sample, deleted immediately.

Excalidraw (eraser/index.ts + App.tsx): on pointer-down it calls eraserTrail.startPath (App.tsx 8114-8118); on each move handleEraser calls eraserTrail.addPointToPath (App.tsx 7536). addPointToPath builds a continuous trail and constructs a lineSegment between the last two trail points (index.ts 96-99), then runs eraserTest against EVERY non-locked candidate element (107-183), accumulating matches into a Set (elementsToErase) rather than deleting topmost-only. eraserTest uses stroke-distance tolerance, freedraw outline-segment distance, filled-shape inside tests, and segment intersection — with an explicit comment (276-279) that it tests against each element segment precisely because fast eraser movement covers large distances between two points. Elements are marked pending and deleted on pointer-up, with alt-key restore support.

The behavioral difference is genuine and user-visible: (1) a fast eraser drag in our port leaves un-erased elements in the gaps between discrete pointer samples, whereas Excalidraw's segment-based test erases everything the continuous path crosses; (2) when multiple elements overlap under a single pointer position, our port removes only the topmost while Excalidraw adds all intersected elements to the erase set. The cited code says exactly what the claim states; nothing is collab/AI/i18n/out-of-scope.

title: Frame created without FRAME_STYLE; uses default black stroke / default roughness instead of grey #bbb, roughness 0
evidence: Our frame: newFrameElement({x, y, width:0, height:0}) — no style. The vendored newFrameElement (newElement.ts:183-198) does not apply FRAME_STYLE, so strokeColor falls back to DEFAULT_ELEMENT_PROPS.black, strokeWidth 2 default, roughness default. Excalidraw spreads ...FRAME_STYLE (strokeColor '#bbb', strokeWidth 2, roughness 0, fillStyle 'solid', backgroundColor 'transparent') plus opacity: currentItemOpacity.
expected: Frame element is constructed with FRAME_STYLE props and currentItemOpacity.
actual: Frame element stores default element style; opacity is the constructor default rather than currentItemOpacity. (Largely masked because the frame border is drawn from FRAME_STYLE by the renderer, so mostly affects stored data / export rather than the on-screen border.)

verdict.reasoning:
The code facts are confirmed. Excalidraw constructs frames at App.tsx:9580-9591 with `{x, y, opacity: this.state.currentItemOpacity, locked: false, ...FRAME_STYLE}`, where FRAME_STYLE (common/constants.ts:196-206) provides strokeColor "#bbb", strokeWidth 2, strokeStyle/fillStyle "solid", roughness 0, roundness null, backgroundColor "transparent". Our caller at draw-controller.svelte.ts:2357 passes only `{x, y, width:0, height:0}`. The vendored newFrameElement (newElement.ts:183-198) does NOT inject FRAME_STYLE; it spreads opts through _newElementBase, which falls back to DEFAULT_ELEMENT_PROPS for any unspecified prop. So our stored frame gets strokeColor COLOR_PALETTE.black ("#1e1e1e") vs "#bbb", roughness ROUGHNESS.artist (1) vs 0, and opacity constructor-default 100 vs currentItemOpacity. (strokeWidth 2 / fillStyle solid / strokeStyle solid / backgroundColor transparent coincidentally match between DEFAULT_ELEMENT_PROPS and FRAME_STYLE, so those do not diverge.)

However the on-screen consequence is nil: the frame border is drawn entirely from the FRAME_STYLE constant in all three renderers — renderElement.ts:804-817 sets context.strokeStyle = FRAME_STYLE.strokeColor and lineWidth = FRAME_STYLE.strokeWidth, and interactiveScene.ts likewise. The element's stored strokeColor/roughness/opacity are never read for frame drawing. So the canvas, SVG, and PNG output are visually identical to excalidraw.

The genuine divergence is confined to persisted/exported element data: serializeAsJSON (file-io.ts:30-42) writes full elements, so a .excalidraw file saved from our port carries strokeColor "#1e1e1e" and roughness 1 on frame elements, differing from a genuine Excalidraw save which carries "#bbb"/0. This is a real data-level divergence (round-trip fidelity, byte-stable export comparison) but produces no visible rendering difference. The opacity divergence is additionally conditional on the user having changed currentItemOpacity. Real but low-impact: data differs, behavior/visuals do not.

title: Resize does not invert aspect-ratio lock for images (shift behaves backwards on image resize)
evidence: Our resize wiring passes `this.#shiftKey` unconditionally as `shouldMaintainAspectRatio` (line 2464). Excalidraw computes it as `selectedElements.some((element) => isImageElement(element)) ? !shouldMaintainAspectRatio(event) : shouldMaintainAspectRatio(event)` — for image elements the aspect lock is INVERTED.
expected: Resizing an image keeps aspect ratio by default (no shift); holding shift frees the aspect ratio. For non-images, shift locks aspect ratio.
actual: Images behave like every other shape: free resize by default, shift to lock. The image-specific inversion is dropped, so image corner-resize distorts the image unless the user holds shift (opposite of Excalidraw).

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading both cited locations and the shared resize logic.

Excalidraw (App.tsx:12653-12668) calls transformElements with shouldMaintainAspectRatio computed as: `selectedElements.some((element) => isImageElement(element)) ? !shouldMaintainAspectRatio(event) : shouldMaintainAspectRatio(event)`. shouldMaintainAspectRatio(event) (common/src/keys.ts:146) returns true when shift is held. So for an image selection, the flag is INVERTED: no-shift → true (aspect locked), shift → false (free distort). This same inversion appears at App.tsx:12434-12436 for the create/draw path, confirming it's intentional and consistent.

Our port (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts:2455-2469) passes `this.#shiftKey` unconditionally as the shouldMaintainAspectRatio argument, with a comment "shift → aspect-locked resize". No isImageElement inversion. So for an image: no-shift → false (free distort), shift → true (locked) — the exact opposite of Excalidraw.

The flag genuinely changes behavior: our port's resizeElements.ts (src/lib/element/resizeElements.ts) is a line-identical copy of Excalidraw's element/src/resizeElements.ts. The multi-element path at line 1306-1318 (`keepAspectRatio = shouldMaintainAspectRatio || ...`) and the single-element path at lines 1002-1014 / 765 / 1112 / 1255 all gate `if (shouldMaintainAspectRatio)` aspect-preserving math on this exact argument. A single selected image (the dominant case) dragged on a corner with no modifier will lock ratio in Excalidraw but stretch/distort in our port; holding shift is likewise reversed.

isImageElement is already imported in the controller (line 41) and used elsewhere (lines 2013, 2058, 2430), so this is a dropped condition, not an out-of-scope feature. Not collab/AI/i18n. This is a genuine, user-visible behavioral divergence on a core image-resize interaction.

title: Resize pointer offset (getResizeOffsetXY) never computed or subtracted — selection jumps on grab
evidence: On pointerdown Excalidraw sets `pointerDownState.resize.offset = getResizeOffsetXY(handleType, selectedElements, elementsMap, origin.x, origin.y)` (App.tsx:8572) and on every move subtracts it: `getGridPoint(pointerCoords.x - pointerDownState.resize.offset.x, pointerCoords.y - pointerDownState.resize.offset.y, ...)` (App.tsx:12591-12592). Our `#beginResize` only stores the handle, originals, and bbox center — it never calls getResizeOffsetXY (which IS ported, resizeElements.ts:490, but unused). pointerMove passes the raw scene `x,y` straight into transformElements (line 2465-2466).
expected: The grabbed corner/edge stays exactly under the cursor; resizing starts smoothly from the point you grabbed.
actual: The element snaps so the handle teleports to the exact cursor position on the first move, producing a visible jump proportional to how far inside the handle hit-region the user clicked.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading all four cited locations. Excalidraw App.tsx:8570-8580 sets `pointerDownState.resize.offset = getResizeOffsetXY(handleType, selectedElements, elementsMap, origin.x, origin.y)` on pointerdown, and App.tsx:12589-12594 subtracts it on every move: `getGridPoint(pointerCoords.x - resize.offset.x, pointerCoords.y - resize.offset.y, ...)` before feeding into the resize. Our port's `#beginResize` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:1960-1968) stores only the handle, deep-copied originals, and the bbox center — it never computes or stores any offset. The pointerMove resize branch (2455-2472) calls `this.#toScene(clientX, clientY)` and passes the raw scene `x,y` straight into `transformElements` as `pointerX/pointerY` (lines 2465-2466). `getResizeOffsetXY` IS ported (resizeElements.ts:490-547) and exported but `grep` shows zero call sites in src/, so it is dead code. Behavioral consequence is genuine: `transformElements` (resizeElements.ts:87-146) treats `pointerX/pointerY` as the new location of the grabbed handle (via getNextSingleWidthAndHeightFromPointer / resizeSingleElement which compute nextWidth/nextHeight from pointer vs anchor). getResizeOffsetXY returns `cursor - anchor` where anchor is the exact geometric corner/edge (e.g. `se` -> (x2,y2)). The handle hit-region (transformHandles.ts:143-172, resizeTest.ts:39-47 isInsideTransformHandle uses the full handle rectangle) is positioned dashedLineMargin+handleMargin+centeringOffset away from that anchor, so a realistic grab lands the cursor several scene-pixels off the anchor with a non-zero offset. Without subtracting it, the first pointerMove forces the handle to the raw cursor position, snapping the element by the offset amount — a visible jump on grab, and the wrong final dimensions are carried thereafter (the offset shifts the entire resize baseline, not just frame 1). This is a real, user-visible divergence. Severity is 'bug' rather than 'partial' because resize is fully functional but every resize starts with an incorrect snap and ends up off by the grab offset; however the magnitude is bounded by the handle region size (a few CSS px / zoom), so it is a small-but-real correctness defect, not a catastrophic break.

title: Marquee (box) selection does not expand partial group hits to the whole group
evidence: Excalidraw runs `selectGroupsForSelectedElements({ editingGroupId, selectedElementIds: nextSelectedElementIds }, ...)` over the box-selected ids (App.tsx:10532) so any element of a group pulls in the entire group. Our `#updateMarquee` calls `this.#setSelection([...this.#marqueeBaseIds, ...enclosed.map(e => e.id)])` with the raw enclosed element ids — no `#withGroupMembers` / group expansion (compare click-select at line 2278 which DOES expand via `#withGroupMembers`).
expected: Box-selecting any member of a group selects all members of that group as a unit.
actual: Box-selecting a sub-region of a group selects only the elements the rectangle actually encloses, splitting the group; subsequent drag/transform moves only the partial selection.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified at both cited locations.

OURS (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts): `#updateMarquee` (lines 1919-1928) computes `enclosed = getElementsWithinSelection(...)` and then calls `this.#setSelection([...this.#marqueeBaseIds, ...enclosed.map(e => e.id)])`. `#setSelection` (1808-1816) is a raw setter — it clears and re-adds exactly the ids passed, with NO group expansion. The lasso path (1957) has the same gap. By contrast, click-select (2278) calls `this.#setSelection(this.#withGroupMembers([hitId]))` and shift-toggle (1820) calls `this.#withGroupMembers([id])`, both of which expand a hit element to all members of its outermost group via `getElementsInGroup`. So marquee is the one selection path that does NOT expand groups.

THEIRS (excalidraw-master/.../App.tsx ~10495-10557): builds `nextSelectedElementIds` from `elementsWithinSelection` (the box-enclosed elements) and then returns `...selectGroupsForSelectedElements({ editingGroupId, selectedElementIds: nextSelectedElementIds }, getNonDeletedElements(), ...)`. That helper pulls in every member of any group that any enclosed element belongs to. So box-selecting a partial sub-region of a group selects the entire group.

Groups are a real, reachable feature in our port: ⌘G grouping (line 977+, addToGroup), ungroup (1004+), and `#withGroupMembers` group-aware selection all exist. So the divergence is not out-of-scope.

User-visible consequence: box-select a portion of a group → ours selects only the enclosed sub-members (splitting the group); Excalidraw selects the whole group. The selection bbox differs, and a subsequent drag/resize/rotate moves only the partial selection in our port vs. the whole group in Excalidraw. This is a concrete behavioral and result divergence for a realistic action (marquee over part of a grouped cluster).

title: Lasso selection does not expand partial group hits to the whole group
evidence: Same group-expansion gap as marquee: `#updateLasso` ends with `this.#setSelection([...this.#lassoBaseIds, ...enclosed])` using raw element ids and no group expansion, whereas Excalidraw funnels all box/lasso-selected ids through `selectGroupsForSelectedElements`.
expected: Lasso-enclosing any member of a group selects the whole group.
actual: Lasso selects only the individually enclosed members, breaking the group apart.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified by reading both cited locations and the helpers each path calls.

OUR PORT (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts):
- `#updateLasso` (1937-1957) collects raw per-element ids into `enclosed` (probe-test of each element's bbox corners/center against the polygon) and ends with `this.#setSelection([...this.#lassoBaseIds, ...enclosed])`.
- `#setSelection` (1808-1816) is a pure setter: it clears `selectedIds`, adds exactly the ids passed, and writes `selectedElementIds` = those ids. No group expansion, and it never sets `selectedGroupIds`.
- A group-expansion helper `#withGroupMembers` (1024-1043) DOES exist and DOES expand a partial hit to the whole group, but it is only wired into single-click hit selection (2278) and shift-click toggle (1820) — NOT lasso or marquee.
- Pointer-up for lasso (2646-2651) and marquee (2666-2671) only clears state; there is no post-processing that expands groups. So the per-element selection set during `#updateLasso` is final.

EXCALIDRAW (lasso/index.ts):
- The lasso selection funnels through `LassoTrail.selectElementsFromIds` (90-157), which calls `selectGroupsForSelectedElements(...)` at line 133 and returns its `selectedElementIds` AND `selectedGroupIds`.
- `selectGroupsForSelectedElements` (packages/element/src/groups.ts:65+) gathers the group id of every selected element (102-105) and then adds ALL non-deleted elements belonging to those groups back into `selectedElementIds` (108-152). So enclosing any single member of a multi-element group selects the entire group and marks the group as selected.

Behavioral consequence for a realistic action: with several elements grouped, dragging a lasso (or marquee) so it encloses only one member selects, in Excalidraw, the whole group (and treats it as a group for subsequent drag/transform); in our port it selects only the individually enclosed member, breaking the group apart. This is a user-visible divergence in core selection behavior. (Note the same gap also exists in `#updateMarquee` at 1925-1928, but the claim is specifically about lasso, which is confirmed.)

Severity: a genuine functional bug in selection/grouping, not cosmetic — group membership is lost on box/lasso selection.

title: Double-click on a grouped element does not deep-enter the group (editingGroupId never set)
evidence: Excalidraw's double-click handler: if a group is selected and the hit element belongs to it, it sets `editingGroupId: selectedGroupId` and `selectedElementIds: { [hitElement.id]: true }` via selectGroupsForSelectedElements, deep-selecting the single child. Our `doubleClickAt` only branches to image-crop or line-editor (`this.enterCrop` / `this.enterLineEditor`) and has no group-entry path. `editingGroupId` is read elsewhere (lines 985,1025-1036) but is never assigned by the controller.
expected: Double-clicking inside a selected group enters the group (editingGroupId set) and selects the individual child clicked, so further clicks select members one at a time.
actual: Double-clicking a grouped element never enters the group; the user cannot deep-select / edit individual members of a group via double-click.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed at both cited locations. Excalidraw's handleCanvasDoubleClick (App.tsx:6533-6557) has an explicit group-entry branch: when getSelectedGroupIds returns >0 and the double-clicked hitElement belongs to a selected group (getSelectedGroupIdForElement), it calls store.scheduleCapture and setState with selectGroupsForSelectedElements({editingGroupId: selectedGroupId, selectedElementIds: {[hitElement.id]: true}}), deep-entering the group and selecting just the clicked child. Our doubleClickAt (draw-controller.svelte.ts:2008-2019) only branches to enterCrop (image) or enterLineEditor (linear); it has no group-entry path and never assigns editingGroupId.

Crucially the rest of the deep-selection infrastructure exists in our port: selection model selects whole groups on single-click (#withGroupMembers at 1804/2278) and #toggleSelected (1819-1830) toggles the whole group as a unit — so individual members are unreachable by click. editingGroupId is consumed all over (interactiveScene.ts:1885 draws the group-edit highlight; zindex.ts, frame.ts, duplicate.ts, delta.ts, and the controller's own group/ungroup at 985/1025-1037 read it as a contextual modifier) but is NEVER assigned by the controller — it stays at its appState.ts:49 initial null. The group helpers (getSelectedGroupIds, getSelectedGroupIdForElement, selectGroupsForSelectedElements) are all present in src/lib/element/groups.ts, i.e. the producer gesture is the only missing piece.

This is a realistic, user-visible divergence in a fully supported feature (grouping is implemented: group/ungroup, group-aware selection, group-edit rendering). A user who groups elements and double-clicks a member to edit it individually gets nothing (or line/crop behavior) in our port, whereas Excalidraw enters the group and selects that single child. Not collab/AI/i18n out-of-scope. The reviewer's reading of both code paths is accurate.

title: selectedGroupIds is never populated (selectAll and all selection paths omit group ids)
evidence: Excalidraw's selectAll (and every selection setter) runs `selectGroupsForSelectedElements` to compute `selectedGroupIds` so groups are treated/rendered as a unit. Our `#setSelection` only writes `selectedElementIds`; `selectedGroupIds` is never set anywhere in the controller (grep returns zero assignments). selectAll just maps every element id with no group bookkeeping.
expected: After selecting (or select-all), selectedGroupIds reflects which groups are fully selected, used for group outline rendering and group-as-unit interactions.
actual: selectedGroupIds stays empty; group membership is only implicit via expanded element ids. Behavior that keys off selectedGroupIds (e.g. the double-click-enter-group check at App.tsx:6535) cannot trigger, and group selection outlines are not driven by it.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed real and user-visible. The port's interactive renderer (src/lib/excalidraw/renderer/interactiveScene.ts) is a faithful copy of master's: line 1813-1818 gives an element an individual selection border only when `locallySelectedIds.has(element.id) && !isSelectedViaGroup(appState, element)`, and lines 1880-1887 draw the single dashed group-outline box by iterating `getSelectedGroupIds(appState)` plus `editingGroupId`. Both of these are driven entirely by `appState.selectedGroupIds`.

In the controller (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts), `#setSelection` (1808-1816) only writes `selectedElementIds` and never `selectedGroupIds`. `selectAll` (888-890), `#select`, `enterCrop`, the click-to-select path (2278 via `#withGroupMembers`), `#toggleSelected`, and even `groupSelected` (978-993, which mutates element.groupIds but never sets appState) all route through `#setSelection`. Grep confirms `selectGroupsForSelectedElements` / `selectGroupsFromGivenElements` are NEVER called by the controller, and there is zero live assignment to `selectedGroupIds` anywhere in the selection path (the only writes are in delta.ts/duplicate.ts restore/merge plumbing and the store default `{}`). So `getSelectedGroupIds(appState)` always returns [] and `isSelectedViaGroup` always returns false.

Master diverges: actionSelectAll.ts:43 spreads `selectGroupsForSelectedElements(...)` into appState (populating selectedGroupIds), and actionGroup sets `selectedGroupIds: {[newGroupId]: true}`.

Concrete user-visible result: select (or select-all) a group in the port and you see N individual element selection borders and NO single dashed group bounding box; in real Excalidraw you see one dashed group outline and no per-element borders (because isSelectedViaGroup suppresses them). After ⌘G grouping, the port draws no group outline at all. This is a genuine rendering/behavior difference for a realistic action (selecting grouped elements), not collab/AI/i18n scope. The helper machinery exists in src/lib/element/groups.ts but is simply never wired into the controller's selection setters.

title: Cmd/Ctrl modifier is hard-coded to false for the linear point editor, so grid-bypass during point editing never works
evidence: #linearEvent() builds the event the vendored editor inspects but sets `metaKey: false, ctrlKey: false` unconditionally, even though the incoming `mods` carries real `ctrlKey`/`metaKey`. The (byte-identical) editor reads `event[KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD]` (=='metaKey' on macOS) at lines 323/339/519/534/1066/1217 to decide `event[KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD] ? null : app.getEffectiveGridSize()`.
expected: Holding Cmd/Ctrl while dragging or alt-adding a point bypasses grid snapping (createPointAt receives null gridSize).
actual: Cmd/Ctrl is always observed as not-held, so points always snap to the grid during editing; the user cannot do free placement.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed genuine divergence. Data flow: EditorPreview.svelte (lines 137-141, 165-169) passes the real DOM `e.ctrlKey`/`e.metaKey` into `controller.pointerDown`/`pointerMove` as a `PointerMods` (whose type at draw-controller.svelte.ts:181-190 includes `ctrlKey`/`metaKey`). During line-point editing these reach `#linearPointerDown`/`#linearPointerMove` (lines 2090-2181), which call `#linearEvent(mods)`. But `#linearEvent` (lines 1993-2001) discards the live modifiers and hard-codes `metaKey: false, ctrlKey: false`, only forwarding shiftKey/altKey. The synthetic event is handed to the byte-vendored `LinearElementEditor.handlePointDragging` / `handlePointerDown`, which at linearElementEditor.ts:323, 339, 519, 534, 1066 evaluate `event[KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD] ? null : app.getEffectiveGridSize()`. `KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD` resolves to `"metaKey"` on macOS (keys.ts:39), so the port always reads it as false and always passes the effective grid size to `createPointAt`/`_getShiftLockedDelta` instead of null. `#linearApp().getEffectiveGridSize` (line 1986-1987) returns a non-null grid size whenever `gridModeEnabled` is true, and the user can enable grid via `toggleGrid` (line 417). Net effect: with grid mode enabled, dragging a linear point or alt-adding a point in excalidraw lets the user hold Cmd/Ctrl to bypass grid snapping for free placement; the port never honors that bypass, so points always snap. This is a real, user-reproducible behavioral difference. It is conditional on grid mode being on (off by default), so it does not affect the common case, but when triggered it produces a different placement result than excalidraw. Note: line 1217 cited in the claim was not individually inspected, but the pattern at the 5 inspected sites (323/339/519/534/1066) is identical and the root cause—hard-coded false modifiers—is shared, so the conclusion holds regardless.

title: Controller never updates hoverPointIndex / segmentMidPointHoveredCoords on pointer move, so point-handle and add-a-point hover affordances never render
ourLocation: src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts:2379-2530 (pointerMove early-returns at 2528 when a linear element is selected but not actively dragging)
evidence: App.tsx calls this.handleHoverSelectedLinearElement on every pointer move when a linear element is selected; that method computes hoverPointIndex via getPointIndexUnderCursor and segmentMidPointHoveredCoords via getSegmentMidpointHitCoords and setState's them. Our pointerMove only enters linear handling when #linearPointerActive (an active drag); on plain hover it falls through to `if (!this.#creating) return;`. The renderer (src/lib/excalidraw/renderer/interactiveScene.ts:121,138) reads these fields to draw the highlight, but the controller never sets them outside an active midpoint add.
expected: Hovering a point handle or a segment midpoint while editing highlights it (pointer cursor + highlighted dot), signalling drag/add affordances.
actual: No hover highlight ever appears; the segment-midpoint add dot and point-handle hover indicator are dead because the state fields stay at their constructor defaults (-1 / null).

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed genuine divergence. In excalidraw, App.tsx:7322 and 7497 call this.handleHoverSelectedLinearElement on every pointer move when a single linear element is selected (7322 for the non-editing case under the guard at 7317, 7497 for the editing case). That method (7551-7649) computes hoverPointIndex via getPointIndexUnderCursor and segmentMidPointHoveredCoords via getSegmentMidpointHitCoords and setState's both, which drives the POINTER cursor (7596) and the rendered hover highlight.

In the port, draw-controller.svelte.ts pointerMove (2379-2530) only enters linear handling inside `if (this.#linearPointerActive)` (2405), which is true only during an active point DRAG (set on pointerdown at 2110, cleared on pointerup at 2185). On a plain hover with no button down, execution falls through every guard to `if (!this.#creating) return;` at 2528 and exits without touching the hover fields. Grep confirms handleHoverSelectedLinearElement / getPointIndexUnderCursor / getSegmentMidpointHitCoords are never invoked from the controller; the latter two helpers exist in linearElementEditor.ts but are only called from the pointer-DOWN path (lines 1040/1098). hoverPointIndex/segmentMidPointHoveredCoords are only assigned during handlePointDragging (linearElementEditor.ts:668-669, active drag) and reset to -1/null on pointer-up (740-741); their constructor defaults are -1/null (198-199).

The renderer reads exactly these fields for the hover affordances: renderLinearElementPointHighlight reads hoverPointIndex (interactiveScene.ts:138), renderElbowArrowMidPointHighlight reads segmentMidPointHoveredCoords (121-128), and the midpoint-dot logic at 1730-1737 checks both. Because the controller never sets them outside an active drag, the point-handle hover highlight, the segment-midpoint add dot, and the POINTER cursor never appear on hover — a visible behavioral difference from excalidraw for the realistic action of hovering over a selected/editing arrow or line.

Not out-of-scope (not collab/AI/i18n), not a misread, not benign. Corrected severity is partial rather than full bug: the underlying drag/add interactions still function via the pointerdown path; only the hover affordance (cursor + highlighted dot) is dead, so the feature is degraded but not entirely broken.

title: No re-bind / un-bind when dragging an arrow endpoint during line editing (only fresh-draw binds, bind-only)
evidence: On pointer-up after dragging a linear point, App.tsx runs actionFinalize which calls bindOrUnbindBindingElement (binds AND breaks bindings, honors isBindingEnabled, handles both-ends-dragged) using the dragged point position. Our #linearPointerUp only calls LinearElementEditor.handlePointerUp + #commit() — it never calls bindOrUnbindBindingElement. handlePointDragging returns a `suggestedBinding` (shown during drag) but nothing persists it on drop. Binding on creation goes through bindBindingElement (bind-only, never unbinds).
expected: Dragging a bound arrow endpoint onto a different shape rebinds; dragging it off its shape unbinds; both-ends-dragged breaks both bindings; disabling binding breaks the dragged end.
actual: Editing an arrow's endpoint never updates bindings: it won't bind to a newly-hovered shape, won't unbind when pulled away, and ignores isBindingEnabled.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified at all cited locations; the claim holds.

OUR PORT (draw-controller.svelte.ts):
- `#linearPointerUp` (lines 2184-2198) handles pointer-up after dragging an arrow endpoint during line editing. It calls ONLY `LinearElementEditor.handlePointerUp(...)` + `this.#commit()`. No binding call.
- `LinearElementEditor.handlePointerUp` (linearElementEditor.ts:680-769) only handles loop-closing (polygon) and selection-index bookkeeping; it performs NO bind/unbind. It does not touch startBinding/endBinding.
- `#bindArrowEndpoints` (lines 2609-2628) uses bind-only `bindBindingElement`, and is invoked solely from the fresh-creation branch (line 2734), reached only when `creating` is a newly-drawn arrow. The editing pointer-up returns early at line 2661-2663 and never reaches it.
- The controller imports `bindBindingElement` but NOT `bindOrUnbindBindingElement` (grep of the file shows no call to it). Our codebase DOES contain a faithful `bindOrUnbindBindingElement` (binding.ts:145-222, with unbind via mode===null at line 233 and honoring appState.isBindingEnabled at lines 178/185) — it is simply never wired into the line-editing pointer-up.
- During drag, `#linearPointerMove`→`handlePointDragging` sets `suggestedBinding` (highlight shown), but nothing persists it on drop, matching the claim.

EXCALIDRAW-MASTER:
- App.tsx:10778-10789: when `selectedLinearElement.isDragging`, pointer-up executes `actionFinalize` with the event + sceneCoords.
- actionFinalize.tsx:78-123: calls `LinearElementEditor.handlePointerUp`, then for binding elements (and not on a segment-midpoint) builds `draggedPoints` from the dragged point position and calls `bindOrUnbindBindingElement(element, draggedPoints, scenePointerX, scenePointerY, ...)`.
- binding.ts:145-222: `bindOrUnbindBindingElement` computes start/end strategies and calls `bindOrUnbindBindingElementEdge` for both ends; mode===null breaks the binding (unbindBindingElement, line 233), and it passes `appState.isBindingEnabled`, so disabling binding breaks the dragged end. Both-ends-dragged updates both bindings.

Net behavioral divergence for a realistic action: in excalidraw, dragging a bound arrow endpoint onto a different shape rebinds, pulling it off its shape unbinds, dragging both ends breaks both bindings, and isBindingEnabled is honored. In our port, editing an existing arrow's endpoint NEVER updates bindings (no rebind, no unbind, ignores isBindingEnabled). This is user-visible: the arrow keeps following the old shape / fails to follow the new one.

title: Fresh-arrow binding ignores isBindingEnabled and uses a static bindMode instead of computed inside/orbit
evidence: #bindArrowEndpoints reads `bindMode ?? 'orbit'` and always binds when a shape is hovered, with no `isBindingEnabled` check. Excalidraw's strategy returns `{mode:null}` (break) when `!isBindingEnabled(appState)` and computes `inside` vs `orbit` per-endpoint based on whether the point is inside the element / pointInElement, rather than a single appState bindMode.
expected: With binding disabled, a freshly drawn arrow does not bind; bind mode is inside when the endpoint is inside the shape, orbit otherwise.
actual: Fresh arrows bind regardless of the isBindingEnabled toggle, and always use the app-level bindMode rather than the geometric inside/orbit decision.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed real, observable divergence on the inside/orbit half; the isBindingEnabled half is real-in-code but latent.

Our fresh-arrow binding path is #bindArrowEndpoints (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts:2610-2628), called unconditionally at line 2734 when a linear arrow finishes drawing. It hand-rolls binding: `const mode = this.appState.current.bindMode ?? "orbit"` (line 2621) applied to BOTH start and end, calling the low-level mutator bindBindingElement directly. It does NOT use our own faithfully-ported strategy machinery (bindOrUnbindBindingElements / getBindingStrategyForDraggingBindingElementEndpoints_simple) that lives in src/lib/element/binding.ts.

Excalidraw decides inside vs orbit PER-ENDPOINT geometrically: in getBindingStrategyForDraggingBindingElementEndpoints_simple (excalidraw-master/packages/element/src/binding.ts:795-817), `current.mode = pointInElement ? "inside" : "orbit"`, where pointInElement = isPointInElement(globalPoint, hit). This is independent of appState.bindMode (appState.bindMode there is a transient orbit/skip/inside hover-dwell state, type BindMode = "inside"|"orbit"|"skip", used only for focus-point projection, not as the stored binding mode in the simple strategy).

In our port appState.bindMode defaults to "orbit" (src/lib/excalidraw/appState.ts:129) and is never mutated anywhere in the draw-controller or routes (grep for `bindMode:` in src/lib/x and src/routes returns nothing). So a freshly drawn arrow whose endpoint is dropped INSIDE a shape always binds as mode "orbit" in our port, whereas excalidraw stores mode "inside". This is user-visible: in updateBoundPoint, `binding.mode === "inside"` short-circuits to keep the endpoint pinned at the interior focus point (src/lib/element/binding.ts:1775-1783), whereas "orbit" runs the full outline-projection. When the bound shape later moves, the arrow endpoint tracks to a different position (interior-pinned vs orbiting the outline). Reproducible by drawing an arrow ending inside a rectangle, then dragging the rectangle.

isBindingEnabled half: #bindArrowEndpoints has no isBindingEnabled check and the call site is unconditional, unlike excalidraw which gates new-arrow binding behind isBindingEnabled(this.state) (App.tsx:9390) and returns {mode:null} when disabled (binding.ts:664). However, appState.isBindingEnabled defaults to true (appState.ts:72) and is never set false in the port (no Ctrl toggle wired), so this half is currently latent and not independently observable.

Net: at least one half (inside/orbit) is a genuine reproducible runtime divergence today, so the claim is real.

title: Delete-point threshold and post-delete selection diverge: refuses to delete below 2 points and clears point selection
evidence: Our deleteSelected guards `el.points.length - editor.selectedPointsIndices.length >= 2` and otherwise silently returns; on success it sets `selectedPointsIndices: null`. Excalidraw deletes whenever fewer than all points are selected (keeps >=1), deletes the WHOLE element (isDeleted) when all points are selected, and after deleting sets `selectedPointsIndices` to `[selectedPointsIndices[0]-1]` or `[0]`.
expected: Deleting points that would leave 1 point is allowed; selecting and deleting all points removes the element; after a delete a neighboring point stays selected.
actual: A 3-point line with 2 points selected no-ops (would leave 1); selecting all points + delete no-ops instead of removing the element; after any successful point delete the point selection is dropped (null), so a follow-up delete does nothing.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading both cited locations. Our deleteSelected (draw-controller.svelte.ts:1226-1248) guards point-deletion with `el.points.length - editor.selectedPointsIndices.length >= 2` and silently `return`s otherwise; on success it sets `selectedPointsIndices: null`. Excalidraw (actionDeleteSelected.tsx:213-273) does NOT use a "keep >=2" guard: (a) when `selectedPointsIndices.length >= linearElement.points.length` it marks the whole element `isDeleted: true` and clears the editor; (b) otherwise it always calls `LinearElementEditor.deletePoints` (confirmed to exist at packages/element/src/linearElementEditor.ts:1481), so deleting 2 of 3 points leaving a single point IS allowed; (c) after a partial delete it sets `selectedPointsIndices` to `[selectedPointsIndices[0]-1]` or `[0]`, keeping a neighbor selected for chained deletes. All three sub-claims produce distinct user-visible divergences in line/arrow point-edit mode: (1) 3-point line with 2 points selected -> ours no-ops (3-2=1, not >=2), excalidraw deletes leaving 1 point; (2) select-all-points + Delete -> ours no-ops (3-3=0), excalidraw removes the whole element; (3) after any successful point delete ours drops selection to null so a follow-up Delete does nothing, excalidraw keeps a neighbor selected so repeated Delete keeps removing points. deletePoints is already imported/used in our port, so only the guard and post-delete selection differ. This is a functional bug, not benign or out-of-scope (point editing is in-scope).

title: Arrow type change to/from elbow does not reposition x/y, reset angle, rebuild points, or recompute bindings
evidence: Excalidraw's changeArrowType, when value===elbow, sets `x=startPoint[0], y=startPoint[1], angle=0`, rebuilds `points` from start/end global coords relative to the new origin, sets `fixedSegments=null`, calls dismissLinearEditor, and recomputes start/end bindings via calculateFixedPointForElbowArrowBinding; for non-elbow it rebuilds points from start/end and re-binds via bindBindingElement(bindMode). Our setArrowType only sets `{elbowed:true, roundness:null, fixedSegments:[]}` then updateElbowArrowPoints with the OLD `elbow.points`; for elbow->non-elbow it only sets `{elbowed:false, roundness}` keeping the routed multi-point geometry, never repositions x/y, never resets angle, never recomputes/re-applies bindings, and uses `[]` instead of `null` for fixedSegments.
expected: Converting a rotated or non-origin arrow to elbow re-anchors it (x/y to start, angle 0) and rebuilds clean orthogonal points with refreshed bindings; converting elbow->sharp/round collapses back to a clean 2-point line.
actual: Converting an arrow with non-zero angle or non-origin start to elbow produces wrong geometry; converting elbow back to sharp/round leaves the jagged routed points; bound arrows keep stale fixed-point bindings after either conversion.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified both cited locations. Excalidraw's actionChangeArrowType (actionProperties.tsx:1806-1965) computes global start/end via LinearElementEditor.getPointAtIndexGlobalCoordinates, then for value===elbow sets x=startPoint[0], y=startPoint[1], angle=0, rebuilds points from global coords relative to the new origin (pointFromAbsoluteCoords with x/y/angle reset), sets fixedSegments=null, calls app.dismissLinearEditor(), and recomputes start/end fixed-point bindings via calculateFixedPointForElbowArrowBinding before passing them into updateElbowArrowPoints. For non-elbow it rebuilds points to a clean 2-point line (the `value===elbow || el.elbowed` branch fires when converting FROM elbow too) and re-binds via bindBindingElement.

Our setArrowType (draw-controller.svelte.ts:781-821): for sharp/round->elbow it only sets {elbowed:true, roundness:null, fixedSegments:[]} then updateElbowArrowPoints with the OLD el.points (relative to the OLD x/y), never repositioning x/y to the global start. mutateElement.ts:53-72 only forces angle:0 (because fixedSegments is defined), but never sets x=startPoint[0]/y=startPoint[1] and never recomputes bindings. updateElbowArrowPoints (elbowArrow.ts:907+) operates on local points relative to the arrow's current x/y; it does not re-derive geometry from global rotated coordinates. So a rotated arrow (angle!=0) loses its rotation but keeps unrotated local points -> visual jump; bindings stay as non-fixed-point bindings rather than being converted to elbow fixedPoint bindings.

For elbow->sharp/round, ours calls mutateElement(el, map, {elbowed:false, roundness}) with no points update. The mutateElement elbow branch is skipped (no points/fixedSegments in updates), so the multi-point routed elbow waypoints are retained verbatim, producing a jagged line instead of Excalidraw's clean 2-point collapse. This is the clearest user-visible bug.

The claim that ours uses [] instead of null for fixedSegments is also accurate (draw-controller line 798 vs excalidraw line 1861). All three claimed divergences (no x/y re-anchor + wrong points for rotated/drifted arrows, jagged retained geometry on elbow->non-elbow, stale/unconverted bindings both directions) match the actual code. Common non-rotated origin-at-start unbound arrows may come out visually equivalent, but rotated arrows, bound arrows, and every elbow->sharp/round conversion diverge.

title: After finalizing a drawn line/arrow, the element is not selected and no LinearElementEditor is created
evidence: Excalidraw's pointer-up for a dragged linear element sets `selectedElementIds: {[newElement.id]: true}` and `selectedLinearElement: new LinearElementEditor(newElement, map)` (when the tool is not locked). Our pointerUp ends with `this.#commit(); this.activeTool = 'selection';` and never selects `creating` nor sets selectedLinearElement.
expected: After drawing a line/arrow, it becomes selected with its point-edit handles available.
actual: The freshly drawn element is left unselected with no point handles; the user must click it again to interact.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified by reading both cited locations.

OUR PORT (draw-controller.svelte.ts):
- pointerDown for a new shape calls `this.#select(null)` (line 2319), clearing selection before drawing.
- The finalize block in pointerUp (lines 2700-2753) sets `this.#creating = null`, runs binding/frame logic, then ends with `this.#commit(); this.activeTool = "selection";` (2749-2752). For the `mode === "linear"` / generic case it NEVER calls `this.#select(creating.id)` and never sets `selectedLinearElement`. Selection is set only for the embeddable path (2716) and via marquee/click elsewhere. `#commit` (319-330) only persists state and does not touch selection.
- `selectedLinearElement` / `new LinearElementEditor(...)` is created only in `enterLineEditor()` (2030-2036), which is reached via double-click (`doubleClickAt` -> `enterLineEditor`), not on draw finalize.
Net: after drawing a line/arrow, `selectedElementIds` is empty and `selectedLinearElement` is null. No point handles; the user must click the element again (and double-click to point-edit).

EXCALIDRAW (App.tsx):
- For a dragged linear element the branch at 10926-10960 runs: when `!activeTool.locked` it sets `selectedElementIds: { [newElement.id]: true }` (10941-10947) AND `selectedLinearElement: new LinearElementEditor(newElement, map)` (10948-10951), then returns at 10961. So the freshly drawn line/arrow is selected with its LinearElementEditor active (point handles available).
- (Generic shapes are likewise selected after draw at 11519-11533, so our port also fails to select generic shapes — a broader divergence than claimed, but the line/arrow claim is exactly accurate.)

This is a genuine, user-visible behavioral difference on a core, high-frequency interaction. The cited code says exactly what the claim states.

title: addMidpoint always snaps to grid (shouldSnap hard-coded true) ignoring Cmd/Ctrl
evidence: Our #linearPointerMove calls `LinearElementEditor.addMidpoint(editor, {x,y}, app, true, scene)` with the 4th `shouldSnap` arg literally `true`. App.tsx passes `!event[KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD]`.
expected: Holding Cmd/Ctrl while dragging out a new segment midpoint places it without grid snap.
actual: A newly added midpoint always snaps to the grid; Cmd/Ctrl free-placement is impossible (compounded by the #linearEvent ctrl/meta=false bug).

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed at both cited locations. Our `#linearPointerMove` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:2141-2147) calls `LinearElementEditor.addMidpoint(editor, {x,y}, app, true, scene)` with the 4th `snapToGrid` arg literally `true`. Excalidraw's App.tsx:9829-9835 passes `!event[KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD]`, i.e. snapping is disabled when Cmd/Ctrl is held. The 4th arg flows directly into `addMidpoint` (linearElementEditor.ts:1690,1715): `snapToGrid && !isElbowArrow(element) ? app.getEffectiveGridSize() : null` is passed to `createPointAt`. So `true` forces the grid size to be used; excalidraw's `false` (when modifier held) forces `null`.

Observability gate: `getEffectiveGridSize()` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:1986-1987) returns `gridSize` only when `gridModeEnabled` is true, else `null`. Default is `gridModeEnabled: false` (appState.ts:71). So when grid mode is OFF (default), both branches resolve to `null` and there is no divergence. When the user toggles grid mode ON (toggle exists at line 417) and holds Cmd/Ctrl while dragging out a new segment midpoint, excalidraw places the midpoint free (unsnapped) while our port always snaps it to the grid. This is a real, user-reproducible behavioral difference.

Compounding bug confirmed too: `#linearEvent` (line 1997-1998) hard-codes `metaKey: false, ctrlKey: false`, so the controller cannot forward Cmd/Ctrl through events on the linear paths — though for this specific call site the snap value is hard-coded `true` rather than derived from the event, so the immediate cause is the literal `true`. Note the in-source excalidraw path for click-adding a point (linearElementEditor.ts:1066) correctly does `event[KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD] ? null : app.getEffectiveGridSize()`, underscoring that the controller-level midpoint-drag call was ported incorrectly.

Not out of scope (no collab/AI/i18n). It is a genuine divergence but conditional: requires grid mode enabled AND modifier held, so it is an edge-case functional bug rather than an always-on break.

title: Linear discard test uses width<1 && height<1 instead of Excalidraw's isInvisiblySmallElement
evidence: Our discard condition is `(mode==='generic'||mode==='linear') && creating.width<1 && creating.height<1`. Excalidraw uses isInvisiblySmallElement, which for linear elements is `points.length<2 || (2 points && arrow && endpoints equal within INVISIBLY_SMALL_ELEMENT_SIZE=0.1)`. For a non-arrow line with 2 points it is never invisibly-small.
expected: A near-zero-length arrow (<0.1px) is discarded; a zero-length line (2 equal points) is kept; a thin but long arrow/line (one dimension large) is kept.
actual: A zero-length line gets discarded (Excalidraw would keep it), and an arrow up to ~1px in both dims is discarded using a 1px threshold rather than the 0.1px endpoint-equality test.

verdict.reasoning:
Both cited locations say what the claim reports. Port draw-controller.svelte.ts:2723-2727 discards a freshly-created generic/linear element when `creating.width < 1 && creating.height < 1`. Excalidraw's isInvisiblySmallElement (sizeHelpers.ts:30-47) instead returns, for linear/freedraw, `points.length < 2 || (2 points && isArrow && pointsEqual(p0, pLast, 0.1))`, and for non-linear `width===0 && height===0`. App.tsx:10986-11004 + actionFinalize.tsx:141/157/231 are the call sites.

So the discard predicates genuinely differ:
- Threshold: port uses a 1px AABB test on both dims; Excalidraw uses a 0.1px endpoint-equality test (arrows only). A sub-pixel diagonal drag of, say, 0.5px in each axis is discarded by the port (both <1) but is NOT invisibly-small by Excalidraw's test (endpoint distance ~0.7 > 0.1).
- Non-arrow lines: isInvisiblySmallElement never flags a 2-point non-arrow line as invisibly small, whereas the port discards a zero-size line.

However the practical, user-visible impact is small/edge-case for two reasons grounded in the surrounding code: (1) Excalidraw gates linear creation with MINIMUM_ARROW_SIZE=20 and DRAGGING_THRESHOLD=10 (constants.ts:19-20) and routes any drag <20px into multi-point mode (App.tsx:10884-10925) rather than finalizing — so sub-pixel/sub-1px arrows are never finalized as 2-point elements there anyway; the 0.1 threshold is just a safety net in the multipoint/finalize path. (2) The port has NO multi-point creation flow and NO MINIMUM_ARROW_SIZE (grep found neither), so its linear creation is a simpler drag-to-2-points model where the only realistic effect of the threshold is that a sub-1px scribble-drag is discarded — which is reasonable and not perceptibly wrong. The 'zero-length line kept' scenario requires a contrived two-clicks-at-identical-pixel multipoint gesture the port doesn't even support. The genuinely larger divergence (port keeps 1-20px tiny arrows that Excalidraw would route into multipoint mode) is caused by the missing MINIMUM_ARROW_SIZE/multipoint flow, not by the discard threshold this claim targets. So the cited threshold difference is real but its observable result is confined to a sub-pixel band and contrived cases.

title: Double-click on canvas/element does not create or edit text at all
evidence: Our doubleClickAt only handles image-crop and line-editor: `if (el && isImageElement(el)) { this.enterCrop(id); return; } this.enterLineEditor();`. There is NO call to start text editing. Excalidraw's handleCanvasDoubleClick, after the linear/image/group cases, calls getTextBindableContainerAtPosition + startTextEditing for the empty-canvas, existing-text, and container-binding cases.
expected: Double-clicking empty canvas creates a new free text element and opens the editor; double-clicking an existing text edits it; double-clicking a text-bindable shape (rect/ellipse/diamond/arrow) creates/edits bound container text centered in the shape.
actual: Double-click does nothing unless the target is an image (crop) or a single selected linear element (point editor). No text is ever created or edited via double-click, despite the HintViewer telling the user 'add text by double-clicking anywhere with the selection tool' (draw-controller.svelte.ts:1755).

verdict.reasoning: Verified by reading both cited locations. Our `doubleClickAt` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:2008-2019) does exactly what the claim says: `const id = this.#hitTest(x, y); if (id) { ...if (isImageElement(el)) { this.enterCrop(id); return; } } this.enterLineEditor();`. It handles ONLY image-crop and the linear point-editor. There is no call to start text creation or text editing. Excalidraw's handleCanvasDoubleClick (App.tsx:6406-6609) handles linear/elbow/image/group/iframe cases and then, for the common path, calls `this.getTextBindableContainerAtPosition(sceneX, sceneY)` and `this.startTextEditing({ sceneX, sceneY, insertAtParentCenter: !event.altKey, container })` — which creates free text on empty canvas, edits existing text, or creates/edits bound container text centered in a text-bindable shape (centering via getContainerCenter). The divergence is genuine and user-visible: in our port, double-clicking empty canvas or a rectangle does nothing, whereas in Excalidraw it opens the text editor. This is not out-of-scope (no collab/AI/i18n) — text creation infrastructure already exists in the port (editingTextId state, textarea overlay in Canvas.svelte, newTextElement at line 2291) and is reachable via the text tool's single-click, but double-click never routes into it. The HintViewer at line 1755 even promises 'you can also add text by double-clicking anywhere with the selection tool', which the double-click handler does not deliver — a self-contradicting gap. I confirmed /x route (EditorPreview.svelte) is the port in question and its ondblclick calls controller.doubleClickAt; the legacy ui/Canvas.svelte is a separate older controller. This is a missing core text-editing entry point, not benign or equivalent.

title: No keyboard path (Enter) to edit a selected text element or container
evidence: The page keydown handler bails early: `if (isTypingTarget(e.target) || editor.editingTextId) return;` and has no branch that starts text editing for a selected element. Excalidraw, on Enter with a single selected element, does `if (isTextElement(selectedElement) || isValidTextContainer(selectedElement)) { ... this.startTextEditing({ sceneX, sceneY, container }); event.preventDefault(); return; }`.
expected: Pressing Enter while a text element or text-bindable container is selected enters the in-place editor centered on the element.
actual: Enter does nothing for text; the only way intended to edit text (double-click) is also broken, so editing existing text is effectively unreachable.

verdict.reasoning:
