The primary claim is confirmed: the active main-app keydown handler `onKeydown` in src/routes/+page.svelte (lines 292-510) bails early at line 293 (`if (isTypingTarget(e.target) || editor.editingTextId) return;`) and contains NO branch that handles the Enter key. I read the entire handler through its closing brace at line 510 — there is no `e.key === 'Enter'` case anywhere. Excalidraw's App.tsx:5218-5239 does handle Enter: on a single selected element, if `isTextElement(selectedElement) || isValidTextContainer(selectedElement)`, it computes the container center and calls `this.startTextEditing({ sceneX, sceneY, container })` then `event.preventDefault()`. So pressing Enter on a selected text element opens the in-place editor in Excalidraw but does nothing in our port — a genuine, user-visible behavioral divergence. The port even has the machinery (`editor.beginTextEdit(id)` at editor.svelte.ts:964) that an Enter branch could call, but it is never wired to a key.

However, the claim OVERSTATES severity. Its assertion that 'the only way intended to edit text (double-click) is also broken, so editing existing text is effectively unreachable' is REFUTED by the code: Canvas.svelte:137 attaches a `dblclick` listener to `onDoubleClick` (line 213-215), which calls `editor.doubleClick` (editor.svelte.ts:990-998); that hit-tests under the point and, for `hit.type === 'text'`, calls `scene.selectOne` + `beginTextEdit`. Nothing indicates double-click is broken — existing text IS reachable via double-click. (Note: the `src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts` controller is a separate /x preview route, not the main editor, so it's irrelevant here.) A separate, smaller gap exists in that Excalidraw's Enter also opens editing on text-bindable containers, while our `doubleClick` only matches `type === 'text'` — but that's not the claim's focus.

Net: a real missing-keyboard-path divergence with a working mouse alternative, so it's a partial functional gap, not the 'effectively unreachable' breakage claimed.

title: In-place text editor (textarea) ignores camera transform — mispositioned when panned/zoomed
evidence: Our textarea uses raw scene coords as CSS pixels: `style="left:{t.x}px; top:{t.y}px; font-size:{t.fontSize}px; ..."`. The sibling embeddable overlay in the same file (line 818-819) correctly converts via `(embed.x + a.scrollX) * a.zoom.value + a.offsetLeft`. Excalidraw's getViewportCoords runs sceneCoordsToViewportCoords (applies scrollX/scrollY/zoom/offset) before positioning the editable.
expected: Editor textarea is placed at the element's on-screen position accounting for scrollX/scrollY, zoom, and offsetLeft/offsetTop; font-size scales with zoom.
actual: Textarea is placed at the element's world coordinates as if zoom=1 and scroll=0. As soon as the canvas is panned or zoomed, the textarea floats away from the element, and the font size never scales with zoom.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed real. In src/lib/x/EditorPreview.svelte:832-848 the in-place text editor is rendered as:

  {#if controller.editingText}
    {@const t = controller.editingText}
    <textarea class="text-editor"
      style="left:{t.x}px; top:{t.y}px; font-size:{t.fontSize}px; line-height:{t.lineHeight};" ...>

`controller.editingText` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:824-831) returns the raw ExcalidrawTextElement straight from the scene map, so t.x/t.y are scene (world) coordinates and t.fontSize is the unscaled font size. These are written directly as CSS pixels with no scrollX/scrollY/zoom/offset applied.

The .text-editor is `position: absolute` inside `.canvas-wrap`, which has no CSS transform (lines 919-923, 955-969), and the canvas DOM layers are full-size statics (camera transform lives in the draw call, not the DOM). So nothing compensates for the missing scene->viewport conversion.

The contrast is in the SAME file: the embeddable overlay at lines 818-819 correctly converts via `(embed.x + a.scrollX) * a.zoom.value + a.offsetLeft` and scales width/height by zoom — proving the transform pattern was available and simply not applied to the textarea.

Excalidraw (App.tsx:5742-5757) passes textWysiwyg a getViewportCoords that runs sceneCoordsToViewportCoords (applies scrollX/scrollY/zoom, subtracts offsetLeft/offsetTop), and textWysiwyg scales the editor font-size by zoom.

Behavioral divergence on a realistic action: with zoom=100%, scroll=0, offset≈0 the textarea lands approximately right, but as soon as the user pans (scrollX/scrollY != 0) or zooms (zoom.value != 1) and then double-clicks to edit text, the textarea floats away from the element and the font never scales with zoom. This is a genuine user-visible mispositioning bug.

title: Element being edited is not excluded from the static render — double-renders under the textarea
evidence: Our renderStaticScene is fed the full `scene.elements` / `getNonDeletedElementsMap()` with no filter for editingTextId. Excalidraw's getRenderableElementsMap explicitly skips it: `// we don't want to render text element that's being currently edited ... if (!editingTextElement || editingTextElement.type !== 'text' || element.id !== editingTextElement.id) { elementsMap.set(...) }`.
expected: While editing, the text element is removed from the rendered scene so only the textarea shows it.
actual: The text element is still painted on the static canvas while the textarea also shows it; combined with the camera-transform bug they appear in two different places.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed at both cited locations.

Excalidraw (excalidraw-master/packages/excalidraw/scene/Renderer.ts:106-114): getRenderableElementsMap explicitly excludes the element being edited from the renderable map — `if (!editingTextElement || editingTextElement.type !== "text" || element.id !== editingTextElement.id) { elementsMap.set(...) }`. This map (sans the editing text element) is what feeds the static scene, so the text being edited is NOT painted on the static canvas while the textarea overlay shows it.

Our port (src/lib/x/EditorPreview.svelte:459-487): the static-scene $effect builds `const elementsMap = scene.scene.getNonDeletedElementsMap()` and `const visibleElements = scene.elements` and passes them to renderStaticScene with NO filter for `controller.editingTextId`. The controller does track `editingTextId` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:206), and `editingText` (line 824-831) resolves it from the same getNonDeletedElementsMap — but the element stays in that map throughout editing (it's only removed on commit if empty, line 855-859). So during editing the text element is still in the static map and gets painted on the static canvas.

The textarea overlay (EditorPreview.svelte:832-848) simultaneously renders the same text. Its CSS (line 955-969) is `background: transparent`, so the static-canvas text shows through. Furthermore the textarea is positioned with raw world coords `left:{t.x}px; top:{t.y}px` with no camera/scroll/zoom transform applied, while the static canvas paints the element through the camera transform — so the two copies land at different screen positions. This is a genuine, realistic, user-visible double-render: start editing any text element and the glyphs appear both on the static canvas (at the camera-transformed position) and in the textarea (at the untransformed position).

Not collab/AI/i18n; not a misread; the cited code says exactly what the claim states.

title: Font-size change does not re-anchor text (no offsetElementAfterFontResize) — text jumps
evidence: #applyTextStyle does `mutateElement(el, ...); redrawTextBoundingBox(el, null, ...)` and nothing else. Excalidraw's changeFontSize calls `newElement = offsetElementAfterFontResize(oldElement, newElement, app.scene)` which adjusts x by `(prevWidth-nextWidth)/(center?2:1)` and y by `(prevHeight-nextHeight)/2`. offsetElementAfterFontResize does not exist anywhere in our codebase.
expected: Increasing/decreasing font size keeps the text visually centered (text grows/shrinks from its center vertically and from its alignment anchor horizontally).
actual: Text stays top-left anchored, so it visibly jumps/grows downward-right on every font-size change.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified both cited locations.

OUR PORT (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts): setFontSize (696-699) calls #applyTextStyle({fontSize}), which (640-654) only does mutateElement(el, map, updates) then redrawTextBoundingBox(el, null, scene) for each selected text element — nothing else. redrawTextBoundingBox (src/lib/element/textElement.ts:62-98) initializes boundTextUpdates.x = textElement.x and y = textElement.y (preserves the original top-left) and only grows width/height (lines 95-98). It never re-anchors x/y for standalone text. offsetElementAfterFontResize does not exist anywhere in src/ (grep confirmed only excalidraw-master has it).

EXCALIDRAW (excalidraw-master/packages/excalidraw/actions/actionProperties.tsx): changeFontSize (251-312) does redrawTextBoundingBox AND THEN newElement = offsetElementAfterFontResize(oldElement, newElement, scene) at 277-281. offsetElementAfterFontResize (230-249) returns the element unchanged only if bound-to-container or !autoResize; otherwise it mutates x (left → prev.x; center → prev.x + (prevW-nextW)/2; right → prev.x + (prevW-nextW)) and y = prev.y + (prevH-nextH)/2. So excalidraw vertically centers the text on every font-size change and horizontally re-anchors center/right-aligned text.

Default text elements in our port are autoResize:true (src/lib/element/newElement.ts:281) and unbound, so excalidraw's re-anchoring branch is active for the common case. setFontSize is wired to the UI (EditorPreview.svelte:716 onFontSize). 

Result: on the realistic action of selecting a text element and changing its font size, excalidraw keeps the text vertically centered (and center/right text horizontally centered) about its prior box, while our port keeps the top-left fixed so the text visibly grows downward (and for left text, only vertical differs; for center/right both axes differ). This is a genuine, user-visible positional divergence. The vertical-centering offset applies for ALL alignments including the default left, so the divergence is not limited to a rare alignment. Not collab/AI/i18n out-of-scope.

title: Font/align changes don't run updateBoundElements — arrow labels don't reposition their arrow
evidence: #applyTextStyle never calls updateBoundElements (the controller only calls it during drag, line 2522). Excalidraw, after changing font size, iterates selected elements incl. bound text and does `updateBoundElements(element, app.scene)`. Same omission applies in setEditingText (834-845) which mutates text but never updates bound elements.
expected: After a font/size/text change to a label bound to an arrow/container, the arrow/container geometry is updated to fit.
actual: updateBoundElements is never invoked from any text-edit or text-style path, so arrow labels and bound containers can desync from their text.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified both cited locations and the supporting code.

THE CLAIM'S STATED MECHANISM IS LARGELY WRONG. Excalidraw's changeFontSize (actionProperties.tsx:290-297) iterates getSelectedElements({includeBoundTextElement:true}) and calls updateBoundElements(element, app.scene) only for isTextElement(element). But updateBoundElements (packages/element/src/binding.ts:1104-1114) early-returns `if (!isBindableElement(changedElement))`. isBindableElement (typeChecks.ts:177-194) returns true for type==="text" ONLY when `!element.containerId`. An arrow/container LABEL has a containerId, so it is NOT bindable, and updateBoundElements is a no-op for it. The loop therefore does NOT "reposition an arrow label's arrow" as the claim states; it only matters for a STANDALONE text element that itself is a binding target of an arrow (arrow endpoint bound to the text). So the claim's headline ("arrow labels don't reposition their arrow") mis-describes what the upstream call does.

HOWEVER, A RELATED REAL DIVERGENCE EXISTS. The actual mechanism that grows a container / re-centers a label after a font/size/align/text change is redrawTextBoundingBox(textEl, container, scene) (textElement.ts:46-140): when `container` is non-null it wraps text to the container's max width, mutates the container's width/height to fit (lines 100-122), and recomputes the label's x/y inside the container (lines 124-137). Excalidraw passes the real container (e.g. actionChangeFontSize line 271-275 via app.scene.getContainerElement(oldElement); actionChangeTextAlign line 1309-1313; actionChangeFontFamily line 1080). Our port passes `null` for the container at EVERY call site: #applyTextStyle (draw-controller.svelte.ts:646), setEditingText (843), #pasteTextElement (1425), pasteStyles (1489), and 2289ff. The controller never imports getContainerElement (grep returns nothing) and never calls updateBoundElements outside the drag path (2522). So for a bound label, changing font family/size/align or live-typing will NOT grow the container or recenter the label — a genuine desync vs excalidraw.

SCOPE LIMITER. The port's own UI never creates bound labels: the text tool (2289-2303) always makes a standalone text element with no containerId, and doubleClickAt (2008-2019) only handles image-crop and line-editor — there is no double-click-to-add-label-to-container/arrow binding. Bound labels can only exist via importing an .excalidraw document that already contains them (the render/bounds/delta code does read containerId/getContainerElement, so such docs load). Thus the divergence is real but only triggers for imported bound-label documents, not natively-created ones.

Net: a real behavioral difference (container/arrow label won't resize-to-fit or recenter on font/style/text change) exists, but the claim's cited cause (missing updateBoundElements) is inaccurate — the true cause is passing null instead of the container to redrawTextBoundingBox — and the user-facing trigger is narrower than the claim implies. Hence partial.

title: Text-style action only touches selected text elements, ignores bound text of selected containers
evidence: #applyTextStyle loops `for (const el of this.selectedElements) { if (isTextElement(el)) { ... } }`. A selected container with bound text is not a text element, so its bound text is skipped. Excalidraw resolves selected elements with `{ includeBoundTextElement: true }` so changing font size with the container selected also changes its label.
expected: Selecting a rectangle/arrow with bound text and changing font size/family/align changes the bound label too.
actual: Bound text of a selected container is never reached; only directly-selected text elements change.

verdict.reasoning:
The claim's code reading is accurate on both sides.

EXCALIDRAW (actionProperties.tsx): All three text-style actions route through selection that includes bound text. `actionChangeFontSize` -> `changeFontSize` -> `changeProperty(elements, appState, cb, true)` (line 287 passes `true`); `actionChangeFontFamily` -> `changeProperty(..., true)` (line 1066); `actionChangeTextAlign` -> `changeProperty(..., true)` (line 1319). `changeProperty` (line 167-188) builds `selectedElementIds` via `getSelectedElements(elements, appState, { includeBoundTextElement: includeBoundText })`, so a selected container's bound text element id is in the set and the callback runs on it. After (line 291-297) it also re-runs `updateBoundElements` over `getSelectedElements(..., { includeBoundTextElement: true })`. Net: selecting a rectangle/arrow with a bound label and changing font size/family/align updates the label.

PORT (draw-controller.svelte.ts): `#applyTextStyle` (640-654) loops `for (const el of this.selectedElements) { if (isTextElement(el)) { mutateElement(...) } }`. `selectedElements` (1706-1710) is `scene.elements.filter(e => selectedIds.has(e.id))` — only DIRECTLY selected elements. A bound text element is not directly selected (only its container is), so it is never in this set and is skipped. The container itself fails `isTextElement`, so nothing changes. `setFontSize`/`setFontFamily`/`setTextAlign` (696-705) all call this single helper. No `includeBoundTextElement`-style resolution anywhere in the controller.

REACHABILITY: The port has full bound-text infrastructure (getBoundTextElement/getContainerElement in textElement.ts, binding.ts, containerId on elements). No controller path CREATES bound text (doubleClickAt at 2008 only does image-crop/line-editor, not double-click-to-label-a-shape; the text tool at 2289 and paste at 1417 both create free-standing text with no containerId; mermaid sets no containerId). HOWEVER, `openFile()` (1163-1173) loads arbitrary `.excalidraw` elements directly into the scene via `replaceAllElements`, preserving any container-bound text. Opening a real Excalidraw file that contains a rectangle/arrow with a bound label is a realistic, supported user action. After opening, selecting that container and changing font size/family/align does nothing to the label in the port but updates it in Excalidraw — a genuine, user-visible (silent wrong result) divergence.

Severity is partial rather than full bug: the action works correctly for free-standing text; it silently no-ops on bound labels of selected containers. The most common in-app creation path for bound text (double-click a shape) is itself not yet implemented in the port, so today the divergence is reachable mainly via imported files, but it is a real wrong-result behavior on a supported code path.

title: Free-text creation does not vertically center the first line on the cursor
evidence: Text-tool click does `newTextElement({ text:'', x, y, ... })` using the raw click point as the top-left. Excalidraw sets `y: sceneY - getLineHeightInPx(fontSize, lineHeight) / 2` so the first line box is centered on the cursor (the comment: 'Free text starts from a point cursor, so center the first line box on it.').
expected: New free text is placed so the click point sits at the vertical center of the first line.
actual: The click point becomes the top edge of the text, so text appears shifted half a line lower than where the user clicked.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified both cited locations. Excalidraw (App.tsx:6262-6281): when creating free text it computes `textCreationGridPoint = getTextCreationGridPoint(sceneX, sceneY)`, which returns `null` whenever no grid is active (getTextCreationGridPoint at App.tsx:1260-1265 returns null when effectiveGridSize is null — the default). In the `textCreationGridPoint === null` branch it sets `y: sceneY - getLineHeightInPx(fontSize, lineHeight) / 2` with the comment "Free text starts from a point cursor, so center the first line box on it." `getLineHeightInPx = fontSize * lineHeight` (textMeasurements.ts:91-96). With default medium font (fontSize 20, lineHeight 1.25) that's a 12.5px upward offset of the element's top edge so the first line box centers on the click.

Our port (draw-controller.svelte.ts:2291-2297) does `newTextElement({ text:"", x, y, ...this.#createStyle(), ...this.#textStyle() })` passing the raw click `y` (the pointer scene-y) directly as the element's top-left y, with no `- getLineHeightInPx/2` adjustment. `#textStyle()` (626-637) only supplies fontFamily/fontSize/textAlign — no y offset. `newTextElement` is imported from the same `@excalidraw/element` package as upstream, so the factory behaves identically; the divergence is solely the missing vertical-center offset in the y argument.

Net effect: for a default-config user (no grid) clicking the text tool on empty canvas, the typed first line appears ~half a line-height (~12.5px at default size, more at larger fonts) lower than where excalidraw places it relative to the cursor. This is a genuine, reproducible runtime positioning difference, not collab/AI/i18n out-of-scope. It is cosmetic in that text is still created and editable, but it is a real visible placement mismatch with upstream.

title: setEditingText does not normalize text or resolve the container for wrapping
evidence: setEditingText assigns `{ text: value, originalText: value }` then `redrawTextBoundingBox(el, null, ...)`. It (a) never resolves the real container (passes null, same bug as #applyTextStyle), and (b) skips normalizeText that Excalidraw's editable.oninput applies before onChange. Excalidraw's updateElement also calls refreshTextDimensions(el, getContainerElement(el), ...).
expected: Live edits normalize the input (tabs/zero-width/line endings) and, for bound text, re-wrap at the container width and grow the container.
actual: Raw textarea value is stored unnormalized and always wrapped as free text, so a text being edited inside a container won't wrap/grow during editing.

verdict.reasoning:
The claim has two halves; only one is a real (minor) divergence.

HALF A — "passes null instead of resolving the real container": REFUTED as user-visible. The port's setEditingText (draw-controller.svelte.ts:834-845) passes container=null to redrawTextBoundingBox, whereas Excalidraw's updateElement (App.tsx:5720-5740) passes getContainerElement(element,...). BUT the port has no code path that puts a bound text element (one with a containerId) into the editing flow. doubleClickAt (lines 2008-2019) only triggers image-crop or the line editor — it never creates text bound to a shape. The text tool (line 2289-2303) and paste (#pasteTextElement, 1409-1432) create standalone text with no containerId. Since getContainerElement returns the element's containerId-resolved container and no editable text element ever has a containerId, the real Excalidraw call would also resolve to null for every editable text in the port. So passing null is behaviorally identical here — latent/dead-code, not a runtime divergence.

HALF B — "skips normalizeText": REAL but narrow. Excalidraw's editable.oninput (textWysiwyg.tsx:615-626) calls normalizeText(editable.value) (textMeasurements.ts:64-70: normalizeEOL + replace /\t/g with 8 spaces) and writes the normalized value back into the textarea AND passes it to onChange on every keystroke/paste. The port's textarea oninput (EditorPreview.svelte:838) passes e.currentTarget.value raw into setEditingText, which stores it unnormalized via mutateElement; measureText (textMeasurements.ts:12-27) does not strip tabs or CR, and commitText (840-863) doesn't normalize on finish either. So pasting text containing \r\n line endings or tab characters into the inline editor produces a stored/measured/rendered string that diverges from Excalidraw (raw \t / trailing \r vs. 8 spaces / \n). The port DOES normalize at element creation (newTextElement -> normalizeText) and when pasting as a new element, so the gap is specifically the live-edit path on an already-created text element.

Realism caveats keep this minor: a <textarea> does not insert a literal Tab on the Tab key (Excalidraw intercepts Tab in onkeydown; the port's textarea onkeydown only handles Escape, so Tab blurs), so the only practical trigger is pasting CRLF/tab-containing content during inline editing. That is a real user action but an edge case.

Net: the container half is benign; the normalization half is a genuine but edge-case behavioral difference.

title: pasteAsPlaintext ignores Excalidraw envelope — always pastes raw JSON as a text element instead of pasting the elements
category: wrong order of operations / missing branch
evidence: Our pasteAsPlaintext does `const text = (await readSystemClipboardText()).trim(); if (text) this.#pasteTextElement(text, ...)` — it never parses the envelope. Excalidraw's plain paste still runs insertClipboardContent, where `if (data.elements)` (line 3762) is checked BEFORE the text branch (3782). parseClipboard (clipboard.ts:541-549) sets BOTH `elements` and `text` when the clipboard holds an Excalidraw envelope, even with isPlainPaste=true. So plain-pasting copied Excalidraw elements re-inserts them as elements (with retainSeed: true), not as a text element.
expected: Plain paste (⇧⌘V) of a clipboard containing the Excalidraw envelope pastes the structured elements (retaining seed), and only falls back to a text element when the clipboard is genuinely non-Excalidraw text.
actual: ⇧⌘V always creates a single text element. If the clipboard holds copied Excalidraw elements, the user gets a text element containing the raw `{"type":"excalidraw/clipboard",...}` JSON instead of the elements.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading both cited locations plus the supporting code.

OUR PORT (draw-controller.svelte.ts:1370-1375): pasteAsPlaintext does `const text = (await readSystemClipboardText()).trim(); if (text) this.#pasteTextElement(text, ...)`. It never calls parseClipboardElements (which exists at clipboard.ts:121 and correctly detects the excalidraw/excalidrawClipboard envelope, and IS used by the regular paste() at line 1353). So ⇧⌘V always creates a single text element via #pasteTextElement (lines 1409-1432: newTextElement with the raw string). If the clipboard holds copied Excalidraw elements, the text element contains the raw JSON envelope.

EXCALIDRAW (App.tsx + clipboard.ts): ⇧⌘V sets IS_PLAIN_PASTE = event.shiftKey (App.tsx:4963), which flows as isPlainPaste into parseClipboard. parseClipboard (clipboard.ts:541-549) returns BOTH `elements` (the structured array) AND `text` (JSON.stringify of the elements) when the clipboard contains Excalidraw elements, even with isPlainPaste=true. insertClipboardContent then checks `if (data.elements)` at line 3762 — BEFORE the text branch at 3782 — and calls addElementsFromPasteOrLibrary with retainSeed: isPlainPaste (=true). So plain-pasting copied Excalidraw elements re-inserts them as structured elements (retaining seed), not as a raw-JSON text element. Excalidraw only reaches the text/addTextFromPaste branch (3859) when the clipboard is genuinely non-Excalidraw text.

REACHABILITY: pasteAsPlaintext is wired to ⇧⌘V via both a keyboard handler (EditorPreview.svelte:353) and a context-menu item (EditorPreview.svelte:256), so this is a realistic user action.

DIVERGENCE: For the realistic action "copy some Excalidraw shapes, then ⇧⌘V", Excalidraw duplicates the shapes (with retained seed); our port instead creates one text element containing `{"type":"excalidraw/clipboard",...}` JSON. Genuine, user-visible behavioral difference — both wrong content and wrong element type. Not collab/AI/i18n; not benign.

Severity: full bug. The user gets a wrong element type with garbage content instead of the expected duplicated elements. It is not merely cosmetic because the resulting scene content differs materially.

title: Plain paste does not split on newlines into multiple text elements (isPlainPaste handling inverted relative to normal paste)
evidence: Excalidraw addTextFromPaste: `const lines = isPlainPaste ? [text] : text.split("\n");` — a NORMAL paste (⌘V) of multi-line text creates one text element PER LINE (centered, wrapped at maxTextWidth, with paragraph gaps), while plain paste keeps it as a single block. Our #pasteTextElement always creates exactly ONE text element from the entire string for BOTH paste() and pasteAsPlaintext(). So normal multi-line paste produces a single element (wrong) and there is no per-line splitting / centering / wrapping logic at all.
expected: Normal ⌘V of multi-line text → one text element per non-empty line, each horizontally centered on the cursor, wrapped to max(min((x2-x1)*0.5,800),200)px, with empty lines collapsed to single paragraph gaps; ⇧⌘V → single block.
actual: Both paths always produce a single text element containing the full string; no line splitting, no centering on cursor, no width wrapping, no paragraph-gap handling.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading both cited locations.

Excalidraw App.tsx `addTextFromPaste` (lines 4120-4237): line 4158 is exactly `const lines = isPlainPaste ? [text] : text.split("\n");`. For a NORMAL paste (isPlainPaste=false, ⌘V), the text is split on newlines and reduced into one ExcalidrawTextElement PER non-empty line: each is horizontally centered on the cursor (`startX = x - metrics.width / 2`, line 4179), wrapped when wider than `maxTextWidth = Math.max(Math.min((x2 - x1) * 0.5, 800), 200)` via `wrapText` (lines 4154/4171-4172), with empty lines collapsed into single paragraph gaps (lines 4194-4202, `currentY += getLineHeightInPx + LINE_GAP` only if prev line non-empty). PLAIN paste (⇧⌘V) uses `[text]` → a single block.

Our port draw-controller.svelte.ts `#pasteTextElement` (lines 1409-1432): builds exactly one `newTextElement({ text, originalText: text, x, y, ... })` from the entire string, with x/y as the raw cursor scene point (top-left anchor, no centering offset), then `redrawTextBoundingBox`. No `text.split`, no `wrapText`, no `getVisibleSceneBounds`/`maxTextWidth`, no paragraph-gap handling exist anywhere in the controller (grep confirmed). Crucially, BOTH `paste()` (line 1361) and `pasteAsPlaintext()` (line 1373) funnel into the same `#pasteTextElement` — there is no isPlainPaste branch at all. `redrawTextBoundingBox` only recomputes bounds for a multiline text element; it does not split lines into separate elements.

Result for a realistic user action: ⌘V of multi-line clipboard text in Excalidraw yields N separate, centered, width-wrapped text elements with paragraph gaps; in our port it yields a single left-anchored block element containing the embedded newlines. That is a clear, user-visible behavioral divergence. The claim's described inversion is accurate (normal paste should split, plain paste should not), and our port effectively behaves like plain-paste-always for both shortcuts.

title: Pasted text element is not centered on the cursor and ignores text wrapping/measurement
evidence: Excalidraw computes `startX = x - metrics.width/2; startY = currentY - metrics.height/2` (centers each text element on the paste point). Our #pasteTextElement sets the element's top-left directly to the scene point (`x = p.x; y = p.y`) with no measurement-based centering. Excalidraw also caps width via maxTextWidth and sets `autoResize: !isTextUnwrapped`; we always rely on redrawTextBoundingBox with default autoResize.
expected: Pasted text is centered on the cursor (top-left offset by half the measured width/height) and long text wraps at the computed maxTextWidth with autoResize disabled.
actual: Pasted text's top-left is placed at the cursor (offset down-right of the cursor), never wrapped to a max width.

verdict.reasoning: Verified both cited locations. Excalidraw's addTextFromPaste (App.tsx:4179-4189) computes `startX = x - metrics.width / 2; startY = currentY - metrics.height / 2`, centering each pasted text element on the paste/cursor point, and caps width via `maxTextWidth = Math.max(Math.min((x2-x1)*0.5, 800), 200)`, wrapping long text with `wrapText` and setting `autoResize: !isTextUnwrapped`. It also splits non-plain text on "\n" into multiple stacked elements. Our #pasteTextElement (draw-controller.svelte.ts:1409-1432) instead sets `x = p.x; y = p.y` directly — top-left at the scene point, no centering — and creates a single newTextElement with no maxTextWidth/wrapText and default autoResize (true), relying only on redrawTextBoundingBox. Both newTextElement and redrawTextBoundingBox are imported from the real @excalidraw/element package (line 49-58), so the helpers are identical; the divergence is purely in the calling logic. This yields a real, user-visible difference for a common action (paste text): the pasted text appears offset down-right of the cursor rather than centered on it, and a long pasted line becomes one arbitrarily-wide unwrapped element instead of an 800px-wrapped one (and multi-line clipboard text becomes one element instead of several). Not out-of-scope (no collab/AI/i18n). The cited code says exactly what the claim states.

title: copyStyles drops the bound text element of the style source
evidence: actionCopyStyles pushes BOTH the element and, if `hasBoundTextElement(element)`, its bound text element: `copiedStyles = JSON.stringify(elementsCopied)` where elementsCopied = [element, boundTextElement]. Our copyStyles stores only `JSON.stringify([deepCopyElement(element)])` — index [1] (the bound text) is never captured.
expected: When copying styles from a container that has bound text, both the container and its bound text element are stored so paste-styles can style bound text from the source's text style.
actual: Only the container is stored; the bound text style is lost on copy.

verdict.reasoning:
Both cited locations read exactly as claimed. Excalidraw's actionCopyStyles (actionStyles.ts:46-59) builds `elementsCopied = [element]` and, when `hasBoundTextElement(element)`, pushes the bound text element (index[1]) before serializing to `copiedStyles`. Our copyStyles (draw-controller.svelte.ts:1440-1448) does `JSON.stringify([deepCopyElement(element)])` — only the container, never index[1].

The divergence is user-visible and reachable. Bound text (labels on shapes) is a real, supported concept in the port (src/lib/element/textElement.ts, binding.ts, types.ts containerId field). Excalidraw's actionPasteStyles (actionStyles.ts:79-95) reads `boundTextElement = elementsCopied[1]` and, when the *target* is a text element with `containerId`, uses the bound text as `elementStylesToCopyFrom`, transferring fontSize/fontFamily/textAlign/lineHeight from the source's label to the target's label. It also gathers targets via `getSelectedElements(..., { includeBoundTextElement: true })`.

Our pasteStyles (1451-1506) uses only `source = JSON.parse(...)[0]` (the container) for everything, and its text-style branch is gated by `isTextElement(source)`. A container is a rectangle, not a text element, so that branch is skipped entirely — the target label's font is never restyled. Compounding this, our `selectedElements` getter (1706-1710) filters only directly-selected ids and never adds bound text of selected containers, unlike Excalidraw's includeBoundTextElement:true.

Net realistic action: copy styles from labeled rectangle A, select labeled rectangle B, paste styles. In Excalidraw, B's label font/size/align/lineHeight update to match A's label. In our port they do not. The container's own styles (stroke/bg/opacity/roundness) still copy correctly, so the loss is limited to bound-text label styling.

Severity is partial (not bug=full-break): the primary container-style copy works; only the bound-text label sub-feature is lost. Not collab/AI/i18n out-of-scope; not a misread.

title: pasteStyles ignores bound text source and applies container style to bound text instead
evidence: actionPasteStyles reads `pastedElement = elementsCopied[0]` and `boundTextElement = elementsCopied[1]`, then for each target: `if (isTextElement(element) && element.containerId) elementStylesToCopyFrom = boundTextElement;` — bound text targets get styled from the copied bound text. Our pasteStyles only parses `source = JSON.parse(this.#copiedStyles)[0]` and applies it to every selected element uniformly; there is no index-[1] bound-text branch (and copyStyles never stored it). A bound text element therefore receives the container's font/style, not the source bound text's.
expected: Bound text elements in the selection take their font/text style from the copied source's bound text element ([1]); other elements take from [0].
actual: All selected elements (including bound text) are styled from a single source [0]; bound-text-specific styling is never applied.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed via both cited locations. Excalidraw's actionCopyStyles (actionStyles.ts:46-67) pushes the container to elementsCopied[0] and, when hasBoundTextElement(element), pushes the bound text element to elementsCopied[1]. actionPasteStyles (lines 78-95) reads pastedElement=[0] and boundTextElement=[1], selects targets via getSelectedElements(..., {includeBoundTextElement: true}) so bound text children are in the target set, and for each target with isTextElement(element) && element.containerId it styles from boundTextElement[1]; all others from [0].

Our port (draw-controller.svelte.ts:1440-1506) diverges on BOTH halves of the mechanism: (1) copyStyles only ever stores `[deepCopyElement(element)]` — a single-element array; it never checks hasBoundTextElement and never stores index [1]. hasBoundTextElement / getBoundTextElement / includeBoundTextElement appear nowhere in the controller. (2) pasteStyles reads only source=JSON.parse(...)[0] and applies it uniformly; there is no index-[1] branch. Additionally our `selectedElements` getter (line 1706) strictly filters scene.elements by selectedIds, and a bound text element's id is not in selectedIds when its container is selected (bound text is a non-independently-selected child via boundElements/containerId), so our paste-target set never even contains the bound text element.

Realistic divergence: copy style from rectangle-with-bound-text, paste onto another rectangle-with-bound-text. Excalidraw updates the target bound text's fontSize/fontFamily/textAlign/lineHeight from the source bound text. Our port leaves the target bound text completely unstyled — the container is not a text element so the line-1481 text branch is skipped, and the bound text is never in the selection set. Net user-visible result: source bound-text font/size/alignment is not reproduced on the target. The claim's phrasing ("applies container style to bound text") is slightly imprecise (we don't restyle the bound text at all rather than mis-styling it with the container), but the observable outcome — bound-text font/style fails to transfer — matches the claim.

Severity is partial, not full bug: container-level styling (background, stroke, opacity, roundness) still copies correctly across containers; only the bound-text font/text styling is dropped, and only in the bound-text scenario.

title: No copyText / copy-as-SVG actions ported (copyText omitted entirely)
evidence: actionClipboard.tsx defines `copyText` (writes getTextFromElements(selected) to the system clipboard, predicate: some(isTextElement)) and `actionCopyAsSvg`. The controller has copyToClipboardAsPng but no copyText and no copy-as-SVG equivalent; grep of draw-controller.svelte.ts shows neither getTextFromElements nor a copyAsSvg path.
expected: Context menu / command palette offers 'Copy text' (writes the text content of selected text elements to the OS clipboard) and 'Copy to clipboard as SVG'.
actual: Only copy-as-PNG exists; copy-text and copy-as-SVG are absent.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified at both cited locations and the surrounding UI wiring.

EXCALIDRAW (their side): actionClipboard.tsx:254 defines `copyText` (perform writes `getTextFromElements(selectedElements)` via `copyTextToSystemClipboard`; predicate requires `.some(isTextElement)` and `probablySupportsClipboardWriteText`). `actionCopyAsSvg` also exists. Both are wired into the UI: App.tsx getContextMenuItems pushes `actionCopyAsSvg` + `copyText` into BOTH the canvas context menu (lines 12713-12715) and the element context menu (12694, 12733), and CommandPalette.tsx:386 exposes `copyAsSvg`. So a user selecting a text element gets a 'Copy text' context-menu entry, and any selection gets 'Copy to clipboard as SVG'.

OURS (port): draw-controller.svelte.ts has `copyToClipboardAsPng` (1509) but NO `copyToClipboardAsSvg` method and NO copy-text action. The context menu / command palette in EditorPreview.svelte (lines 213-260) offer only 'Copy' (⌘C), 'Copy styles' (⌥⌘C), and 'Copy to clipboard as PNG' — neither 'Copy text' nor 'Copy to clipboard as SVG' is present. HelpDialog.svelte:118 lists only the PNG shortcut.

Nuance on the claim's evidence: it asserted grep shows 'neither getTextFromElements nor a copyAsSvg path.' That part is imprecise — `getTextFromElements` IS ported (src/lib/element/textElement.ts:519) and `copyTextToSystemClipboard` exists (src/lib/x/clipboard.ts:55). However `getTextFromElements` has ZERO callers in src/ and there is no SVG-to-clipboard path, so the utilities are unwired/dead for this purpose. The core claim — that the copyText action and copy-as-SVG action are not ported into the controller or any UI surface — is correct.

This is a genuine, user-visible behavioral divergence: in Excalidraw a user can right-click a text element and pick 'Copy text', or pick 'Copy to clipboard as SVG'; in our port those menu items simply do not exist. It is a missing-feature/parity gap rather than a wrong-result regression of an existing feature, hence 'partial'.

title: saveAs picker error other than AbortError silently falls back to a download (can double-save / mask write errors)
evidence: In saveAsExcalidraw, after showSaveFilePicker succeeds and createWritable/write throws a non-Abort error, the catch (line 97-103) does nothing and execution falls through to the `<a download>` fallback at line 106 — so a failed FS-Access write silently triggers a second download save. browser-fs-access (Excalidraw's fileSave) only falls back when the picker API is unavailable, not when an actual write fails after a handle was obtained; a write error would propagate.
expected: A genuine write failure after obtaining a file handle should surface (or not silently re-save via a different mechanism).
actual: Any non-Abort error during the FS-Access write is swallowed and a download save is triggered as a fallback, masking the original failure.

verdict.reasoning:
Code reading is accurate. In the port (src/lib/x/file-io.ts:75-114), when showSaveFilePicker is supported, the try block acquires a handle, then calls createWritable()/write()/close() (lines 86-95). The catch at line 97-102 only returns false for AbortError; for ANY OTHER error (including a real write failure AFTER the handle was obtained) it does nothing, so execution falls through to the `<a download>` blob fallback at lines 106-114 and returns true (success).

Excalidraw's contract is different. saveAsExcalidraw mirrors data/json.ts:saveAsJSON, which calls browser-fs-access fileSave (via data/filesystem.ts:49-73). browser-fs-access's fileSave only routes to the legacy `<a download>` path when `supported` (FS Access API availability, evaluated at import time) is false — NOT as a catch-fallback. Once showSaveFilePicker succeeds and the handle is obtained, a createWritable/write/close failure rejects the returned promise and propagates to the caller. In actionExport.tsx:391-411 that rejection is caught: it aborts, console.error's the error, and does NOT emit the toast.fileSaved success toast and does NOT trigger any second save. So a genuine post-handle write error surfaces (logged, no success indication) with no duplicate save.

Realistic trigger: write failure after a successful Save-dialog pick (disk full, quota exceeded, permission revoked mid-write, I/O error). In that path the port silently performs a SECOND save by dropping a blob into the Downloads folder and reports success, while upstream surfaces the error and performs no second save. That is a user-visible divergence (stray Downloads file + masked error). It is confined to the rare error path, not the happy path, hence 'partial' rather than 'bug'.

title: copySelected does not surface an error toast / errorMessage when the clipboard write fails
evidence: actionCopy sets `appState.errorMessage = error.message` when copyToClipboard throws. Our copySelected catches and only `console.warn("copy to system clipboard failed", error)` — no user-visible error and no toast. (Mitigated by the in-memory mirror still working, so paste succeeds, but the OS-clipboard failure is invisible.)
expected: A failed OS clipboard copy surfaces an error message to the user.
actual: The failure is logged to console only; the user sees no indication the system clipboard wasn't updated.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified both cited locations. In excalidraw-master/packages/excalidraw/actions/actionClipboard.tsx:35-44, actionCopy wraps copyToClipboard in try/catch and on failure returns `appState: { ...appState, errorMessage: error.message }`. That errorMessage is rendered as a modal via ErrorDialog in LayerUI.tsx:487-489 (`{appState.errorMessage && (<ErrorDialog ...>`), so a failed copy is user-visible.

Our copySelected (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts:1329-1334) catches the same throw and does only `console.warn("copy to system clipboard failed", error)` — no user-visible feedback. Confirmed via grep that our port has NO errorMessage usage in the controller/app-state and NO ErrorDialog component anywhere in src/ (errorMessage exists only as a field in the ported types.ts:282, never wired to rendering). The port does have a Toast mechanism but copy failure does not route to it.

The throw condition is genuinely reachable and faithfully ported: our copyTextToSystemClipboard (clipboard.ts:55-68) mirrors excalidraw's (clipboard.ts:586-635) — it tries navigator.clipboard.writeText, falls back to document.execCommand('copy'), and throws `"Error copying to clipboard."` only when BOTH fail. So for a realistic user action (Cmd+C / Copy menu item, wired at EditorPreview.svelte:213/253/347) in a context where both clipboard paths fail (sandboxed/unfocused document, browsers without execCommand), excalidraw pops an error dialog and our port shows nothing.

This is a real user-visible divergence. Severity is tempered to 'partial' rather than 'bug': the trigger is narrow (both clipboard write paths must fail — uncommon), and the in-memory mirror means intra-app paste still works. The actual user-facing loss is the silent failure to write the OS clipboard, so cross-app paste fails with no warning — a degraded but not broken core flow.

title: Style changes mutate elements in place without invalidating ShapeCache/elementWithCanvasCache, so fill/stroke/width/style/sloppiness/color edits render stale geometry
evidence: Excalidraw's actions build elements via `newElementWith(el, {fillStyle/strokeWidth/strokeStyle/roughness/...})`, which returns a NEW object reference. Both `ShapeCache` (WeakMap keyed by element ref, shape.ts:83) and `elementWithCanvasCache` (WeakMap, renderElement.ts:603) are keyed by reference, so a fresh reference auto-misses and regenerates the rough shape that bakes in fillStyle/strokeWidth/strokeStyle/strokeColor/backgroundColor/roughness (generateRoughOptions, shape.ts:199-243). Our `#applyStyle` calls `mutateElement(el, map, elementPatch)` which mutates IN PLACE (same reference). Our `mutateElement` only calls `ShapeCache.delete(element)` when height/width/fileId/points change (mutateElement.ts:130-137) — NOT for strokeColor/backgroundColor/fillStyle/strokeWidth/strokeStyle/roughness. The per-element canvas cache (renderElement.ts:631-645) regenerates only on zoom/theme/boundTextVersion/imageCrop/frameOpacity/arrow-angle, never on version/versionNonce. Net: after changing fill/stroke-style/stroke-width/sloppiness/colors on selected elements, the renderer keeps drawing the OLD rough geometry until an unrelated resize/move invalidates the cache.
expected: Changing fill style / stroke width / stroke style / sloppiness / stroke or background color on selected elements immediately repaints those elements with the new appearance.
actual: appState defaults update and version bumps, but the cached rough shape and cached element canvas are retained, so selected elements visually keep their old fill/stroke/roughness until another mutation (move/resize) clears the cache.

verdict.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. I read all cited code in both repos.

excalidraw mechanism: actionProperties.tsx style actions (actionChangeFillStyle:478-485, actionChangeStrokeWidth:555-557, actionChangeSloppiness:611-614, actionChangeStrokeStyle:667-669, and stroke/background color) all do `changeProperty(elements, appState, (el) => newElementWith(el, {fillStyle/strokeWidth/...}))`. `newElementWith` (mutateElement.ts:146-178) returns a NEW object via `{...element, ...updates}` whenever a value changed. `changeProperty` (167-188) returns a new elements array with these fresh references for selected elements.

Both caches are reference-keyed WeakMaps and byte-identical across the two repos: `ShapeCache.cache` (shape.ts:83) and `elementWithCanvasCache` (renderElement.ts:603). A new element reference auto-misses both: `ShapeCache.get` returns undefined -> `generateElementShape` regenerates (shape.ts:130-153); `generateElementWithCanvas` sees `!prevElementWithCanvas` -> regenerates (renderElement.ts:631-661). `generateRoughOptions` (shape.ts:193-244) bakes strokeStyle, strokeWidth, roughness, strokeColor, fillStyle, backgroundColor INTO the cached Drawable, so regeneration is required for the new appearance to render.

Our port diverges: `#applyStyle` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:578-601) calls the bare in-place `mutateElement(el, map, elementPatch)` (imported at line 34), which keeps the SAME reference. Our `mutateElement` (mutateElement.ts:130-137) is identical to theirs and only calls `ShapeCache.delete(element)` when height/width/fileId/points change — NOT for strokeColor/backgroundColor/fillStyle/strokeWidth/strokeStyle/roughness. The per-element canvas cache (renderElement.ts:631-644) regenerates only on zoom/theme/boundTextVersion/imageCrop/frameOpacity/arrow-angle — never on version/versionNonce or style props. Neither `Scene.mutateElement` (Scene.ts:411-445), `triggerUpdate` (303-309), nor the controller's `#commit` (draw-controller:319) clears either cache or replaces references.

Net: after changing fill/stroke-style/stroke-width/sloppiness/stroke-color/background-color on already-rendered selected elements, both WeakMaps retain stale entries keyed by the still-live reference; the renderer keeps drawing the old rough geometry until an unrelated move/resize (which passes width/height/points and thus deletes ShapeCache) invalidates the cache. This is a user-visible divergence on the core property panel.

The claim is precisely scoped: opacity is correctly excluded (opacity is applied at draw-time via globalAlpha, not baked into the cached shape), which I confirmed is consistent with the cited generateRoughOptions not consuming opacity. Could not refute any part.

title: setSloppiness does not regenerate element seed, so the sketch does not re-randomize on roughness change
evidence: actionChangeSloppiness writes BOTH `seed: randomInteger()` and `roughness: value` in its newElementWith callback. Our `setSloppiness` only passes `{ roughness }`. The rough shape uses `seed: element.seed` (shape.ts:199), so without a new seed the randomized sketch path is not re-rolled. Combined with the stale-cache bug above, a sloppiness change can produce no visible update at all.
expected: Switching Architect/Artist/Cartoonist re-seeds each selected element so the sketch geometry visibly re-randomizes with the new roughness.
actual: Only `roughness` is mutated; `seed` is untouched, so even after cache invalidation the sketch is not re-randomized.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified at both cited locations and the full cache/render path.

THEIR code (excalidraw-master/packages/excalidraw/actions/actionProperties.tsx:609-619): actionChangeSloppiness's perform maps each selected element through newElementWith(el, { seed: randomInteger(), roughness: value }). It writes BOTH a fresh seed and the new roughness. newElementWith returns a NEW element object.

OUR code (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts:544-546): setSloppiness(roughness) calls #applyStyle({ roughness }, { currentItemRoughness: roughness }). #applyStyle (lines 589-601) calls mutateElement(el, map, { roughness }) — only roughness, no seed. mutateElement is in-place (preserves object identity).

Supporting evidence all confirmed:
- shape.ts:199 and :593 pass seed: element.seed into the rough generator options, so seed drives the randomized sketch geometry.
- ShapeCache (shape.ts:83-112) is a WeakMap keyed purely on element object identity, no version/versionNonce check. It is only invalidated via ShapeCache.delete().
- mutateElement.ts:130-137 calls ShapeCache.delete(element) ONLY when width/height/fileId/points change. roughness is NOT in that list. So a roughness-only mutation does not invalidate the shape cache.
- elementWithCanvasCache (renderElement.ts:619-663) also tracks zoom/theme/boundText/imageCrop/frameOpacity but not roughness/seed/version; it relies on explicit deletion too.
- Scene.triggerUpdate (Scene.ts:303-309) only bumps sceneNonce and fires re-render callbacks; it does not flush ShapeCache. The re-render hits generateElementShape, which returns the stale cached shape for the same (unchanged-identity) element.

Net runtime divergence: In excalidraw, switching Architect/Artist/Cartoonist creates new element objects (WeakMap cache miss) → full regeneration with a freshly randomized seed AND the new roughness, so the sketch visibly re-rolls. In our port, the in-place mutation neither re-seeds nor invalidates either cache, so for already-selected existing elements the rough geometry is not regenerated at all — the sloppiness change can produce no visible update to the sketch. This is a genuine user-visible behavioral difference for a normal action (selecting shapes and clicking a sloppiness button).

Note: newly-drawn elements still get the new roughness via #createStyle (currentItemRoughness), so the bug is specifically about re-styling existing selected elements, which is exactly the cited scenario.

title: setEdges always applies ADAPTIVE_RADIUS; diamonds/lines/arrows should get PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS, and elbow arrows must be skipped
evidence: actionChangeRoundness picks the roundness type per element: `type: isUsingAdaptiveRadius(el.type) ? ROUNDNESS.ADAPTIVE_RADIUS : ROUNDNESS.PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS`, where isUsingAdaptiveRadius (typeChecks.ts:308-312) is true only for rectangle/embeddable/iframe/image — diamond/line/arrow use PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS. It also early-returns `el` for elbow arrows (`if (isElbowArrow(el)) return el`). Our setEdges hardcodes `roundness: value === 'round' ? { type: ROUNDNESS.ADAPTIVE_RADIUS } : null` for every selected element, with no elbow-arrow guard. (Note: the port already does this correctly in the paste-styles path at draw-controller.svelte.ts:1475-1479 via canApplyRoundnessTypeToElement, so setEdges is inconsistent with the rest of the port.)
expected: Rounding a selected diamond/line/arrow applies PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS; elbow arrows are left unchanged.
actual: All elements receive ADAPTIVE_RADIUS regardless of type, giving the wrong corner radius on diamonds/lines/arrows, and elbow arrows are mutated when they should be skipped.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed at every layer. Excalidraw's actionChangeRoundness (actionProperties.tsx:1499-1516) early-returns elbow arrows (`if (isElbowArrow(el)) return el`) and selects roundness type per element: `isUsingAdaptiveRadius(el.type) ? ADAPTIVE_RADIUS : PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS`. isUsingAdaptiveRadius (typeChecks.ts:308-312) is true only for rectangle/embeddable/iframe/image; line/arrow/diamond use PROPORTIONAL (isUsingProportionalRadius, 314-315). The port's setEdges (draw-controller.svelte.ts:558-562) loops over every selected element and hardcodes `roundness: value === 'round' ? { type: ROUNDNESS.ADAPTIVE_RADIUS } : null` with no elbow-arrow guard and no per-type branching. The difference is render-visible: the port's own getCornerRadius (src/lib/element/utils.ts:483-504, byte-identical to upstream utils.ts:483) computes PROPORTIONAL as `x * DEFAULT_PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS` vs ADAPTIVE as a fixed-pixel cap above a cutoff, so a diamond/line/arrow rounded via setEdges gets a materially different corner radius than excalidraw would produce. Elbow arrows also get a roundness mutation they should never receive. The port already does this correctly in its paste-styles path (draw-controller.svelte.ts:1475-1479) via canApplyRoundnessTypeToElement/getDefaultRoundnessTypeForElement, confirming setEdges is an inconsistent outlier, not an intentional design choice. Realistic user action: select a diamond and click round-edges.

title: Stroke color / opacity changes do not propagate to bound text elements (includeBoundText)
evidence: actionChangeStrokeColor and actionChangeOpacity call `changeProperty(elements, appState, cb, /*includeBoundText*/ true)`. changeProperty (lines 167-188) collects selected elements WITH `includeBoundTextElement: true`, so a container's bound text element is also recolored / re-opacified. Our `selectedElements` getter only returns elements whose id is in selectedIds (1707-1709); it never adds bound text children, and `#applyStyle` iterates only that set. So changing stroke color or opacity on a rectangle/ellipse with bound text leaves the text element at its old color/opacity.
expected: Changing stroke color or opacity on a container also updates its bound text element to match.
actual: Only the directly-selected container is mutated; its bound text keeps the previous strokeColor/opacity.

verdict.reasoning:
The cited code says what is claimed, and the divergence is reachable.

Excalidraw side (confirmed): actionProperties.tsx actionChangeStrokeColor (lines ~318-336) and actionChangeOpacity (~714-728) call changeProperty(..., /*includeBoundText*/ true). changeProperty (167-188) builds selectedElementIds via getSelectedElements(..., {includeBoundTextElement: true}). getSelectedElements (selection.ts 427-470) explicitly adds any element that isBoundToContainer whose containerId is in selectedElementIds. So selecting a container and changing stroke color or opacity also mutates its bound text element.

Our side (confirmed): src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts selectedElements getter (1706-1709) returns only this.scene.elements.filter((e) => this.selectedIds.has(e.id)) — it never adds bound text children. #applyStyle (578-601) iterates exactly that set and calls mutateElement on each, so a selected container's bound text is never touched. Stroke color and opacity changes therefore leave the bound text at its old values.

Reachability check (the part that decides benign-vs-real): bound-text infrastructure exists and is wired into the live scene. (1) Our port loads .excalidraw files via openFile() -> openExcalidrawFile() -> parseExcalidrawJSON (file-io.ts 123-133), which preserves data.elements verbatim including containerId/boundElements; openFile (draw-controller 1162-1173) only runs syncInvalidIndices (reorders fractional indices, does not strip binding) before replaceAllElements. So a user opening any real Excalidraw drawing that contains a rectangle/ellipse with bound text gets a live, selectable container whose bound text is a separate text element with its own strokeColor/opacity. (2) src/lib/element/textElement.ts, bounds.ts, binding.ts, transform.ts all implement getBoundTextElement/containerId/boundElements, and the renderer/bounds use the bound text's own strokeColor — so the text rendering really would stay the old color while the container border changes.

Note: there is currently no controller-level user action to CREATE a binding from scratch (doubleClickAt at 2008 only enters image-crop or the line editor; the text tool at 2289 and addText at 1417 create free-standing text with containerId:null). But because opened files preserve bindings, the realistic action "open an .excalidraw with bound text, select the container, change stroke color / opacity" produces a different, user-visible result than excalidraw-master. That makes this a genuine behavioral divergence rather than benign/unreachable.

Severity is partial: the common case (drawings created in our port, which can't yet bind text) is unaffected; the divergence manifests only on imported files that contain pre-existing bound text. When it does manifest it is a real wrong result (bound text keeps stale color/opacity), not merely cosmetic.

title: #applyStyle applies strokeColor to element types that have no stroke color (image/frame), ignoring the hasStrokeColor guard
evidence: actionChangeStrokeColor only mutates when `hasStrokeColor(el.type)` is true; otherwise returns el unchanged. hasStrokeColor (comparisons.ts:12-20) excludes image, frame, iframe. Our setStrokeColor → #applyStyle mutates strokeColor on every selected element unconditionally, so selecting an image/frame and picking a stroke color writes a strokeColor onto it (and bumps its version) where Excalidraw is a no-op.
expected: Stroke color is only written to element types that have a stroke (rectangle/ellipse/diamond/freedraw/arrow/line/text/embeddable); images/frames are skipped.
actual: strokeColor is written to all selected elements including images/frames.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed at both cited locations and verified the runtime path.

EXCALIDRAW SIDE (actionProperties.tsx:322-336): `actionChangeStrokeColor.perform` maps over elements via `changeProperty` and only mutates when `hasStrokeColor(el.type)` is true, otherwise returns `el` unchanged. `hasStrokeColor` (excalidraw-master/packages/element/src/comparisons.ts:12-20) returns true only for rectangle/ellipse/diamond/freedraw/arrow/line/text/embeddable — image, frame, iframe, magicframe are excluded. Additionally, the stroke-color panel visibility itself is guarded in Actions.tsx:118-124 (`canChangeStrokeColor`): for a selection that is purely image/frame/magicframe, the panel does not render at all (active 'selection' tool has no stroke color, and `targetElements.some(hasStrokeColor)` is false).

OUR SIDE: `setStrokeColor` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:512-513) calls `#applyStyle({strokeColor}, ...)`. `#applyStyle` (591-598) iterates over `this.selectedElements` and calls `mutateElement(el, map, {strokeColor})` on EVERY selected element with no `hasStrokeColor` type guard. `selectedElements` (1706-1710) is an unfiltered filter over scene elements, so it includes images and frames. `mutateElement` (mutateElement.ts:77-90) writes the prop and bumps version whenever the new value differs from the element's current strokeColor (images carry a strokeColor field, so a new color differs and is written). The UI panel (EditorPreview.svelte:583-604) renders the Stroke prop-group unconditionally (gated only by zen/view mode), so the swatch is clickable even when only an image/frame is selected.

So two genuine divergences arise from realistic actions: (1) selecting a lone image/frame shows the Stroke picker in our port and lets the user write strokeColor onto it (version bump), whereas excalidraw shows no stroke panel and never mutates; (2) in a mixed selection (rectangle + image), both show the panel, but excalidraw skips the image while ours writes strokeColor to it and bumps its version. The reviewer's read of the code is accurate.

Severity: partial rather than bug. strokeColor on an image/frame is never rendered, so the document's visual output is unchanged; the observable effects are an extra version bump (affects undo-snapshot/scene-change detection) and the Stroke panel being visible for image/frame selections where excalidraw hides it. Real and user-observable, but it does not corrupt rendered output or produce a wrong export.

title: setBackgroundColor drops the line→polygon enable step
evidence: actionChangeBackgroundColor computes `shouldEnablePolygon = !isTransparent(color) && selectedElements.every(el => isLineElement(el) && canBecomePolygon(el.points))` and, when true, applies `...toggleLinePolygonState(el, true)` alongside the backgroundColor so a closeable line becomes a filled polygon. Our setBackgroundColor is a plain `#applyStyle({ backgroundColor })` with no polygon handling, so giving a closed line a non-transparent fill never flips it into polygon state.
expected: Applying a non-transparent background to selected closeable line elements enables polygon (fills the shape).
actual: backgroundColor is set but the line is never converted to a polygon.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified at both cited locations. Excalidraw's actionChangeBackgroundColor (actionProperties.tsx:397-421) computes shouldEnablePolygon = !isTransparent(color) && selectedElements.every(el => isLineElement(el) && canBecomePolygon(el.points)); when true it maps selected line elements through newElementWith(el, { backgroundColor, ...toggleLinePolygonState(el, true) }), converting a closeable line into a filled polygon. Our port's setBackgroundColor (draw-controller.svelte.ts:515-516) calls #applyStyle({ backgroundColor }, ...), and #applyStyle (lines 578-601) is a plain loop of mutateElement(el, map, elementPatch) with no polygon branch — its elementPatch type doesn't even include polygon. mutateElement.ts has no polygon/backgroundColor side effects, so polygon is never enabled.

The required helpers exist in the port (src/lib/element/typeChecks.ts:386 canBecomePolygon, src/lib/element/shape.ts:1126 toggleLinePolygonState — the latter already used elsewhere, e.g. linearElementEditor.ts:710), confirming polygons are a live feature, not out-of-scope. The path is user-reachable: the line tool exists (EditorPreview.svelte: 'line' tool, shortcut L) and the background swatch calls controller.setBackgroundColor(c) (EditorPreview.svelte:627). Rendering treats non-polygon lines as unfilled (bounds.ts:323 isLineElement && !element.polygon), so a user who draws a closed/closeable line, selects it, and picks a non-transparent background sees NO fill in the port, whereas excalidraw fills it as a polygon. Genuine user-visible divergence.

Not benign, not a misread, not out-of-scope (no collab/AI/i18n). Severity is partial rather than full bug because setBackgroundColor works correctly for all elements in the normal case; only the narrow line→polygon auto-conversion sub-behavior (closeable line + non-transparent color) is missing.

title: Zoom-in/zoom-out use a multiplicative factor (×1.1) instead of Excalidraw's additive ZOOM_STEP (+/-0.1)
evidence: Our footer buttons call `controller.zoomAt(1.1, ...)` / `zoomAt(1/1.1, ...)`, and zoomAt computes `nz = clamp(z * factor)`. Excalidraw's actionZoomIn/Out compute `getNormalizedZoom(appState.zoom.value + ZOOM_STEP)` with `ZOOM_STEP = 0.1` (additive).
expected: Clicking zoom-in goes 1.0 → 1.1 → 1.2 (additive 0.1 steps, landing on round percentages like 100%, 110%, 120%).
actual: Clicking zoom-in goes 1.0 → 1.1 → 1.21 → 1.331 (multiplicative), producing non-round zoom percentages (e.g. 121%, 133%) that drift from Excalidraw's grid.

verdict.reasoning: Both cited locations say what the claim states. Excalidraw's actionZoomIn/actionZoomOut (actionCanvas.tsx:148,189) compute nextZoom = getNormalizedZoom(appState.zoom.value ± ZOOM_STEP) with ZOOM_STEP = 0.1 (common/src/constants.ts:302). getNormalizedZoom (scene/normalize.ts:7-9) only does clamp(round(zoom,6), 0.1, 30) — no multiplicative snapping — so button zoom is genuinely additive: 1.0→1.1→1.2→1.3 (100%, 110%, 120%, 130%). Our footer buttons (EditorPreview.svelte:853,857) call controller.zoomAt(1.1,…) and zoomAt(1/1.1,…), and zoomAt (draw-controller.svelte.ts:390-401) computes nz = Math.min(30, Math.max(0.1, z * factor)) — multiplicative. So our zoom-in sequence is 1.0→1.1→1.21→1.331 (100%, 110%, 121%, 133%). The displayed footer percentage (Math.round(zoom*100)%) diverges starting from the second click (121% vs 120%), and the underlying zoom values drift from Excalidraw's grid. This is a real, user-visible runtime divergence for a realistic action (repeatedly clicking the footer +/- zoom buttons). Clamp ranges (0.1–30) match. Not collab/AI/i18n; reviewer did not misread.

title: zoomAt does not normalize zoom via getNormalizedZoom (no round-to-6-dp), so floating-point dust accumulates
evidence: zoomAt: `const nz = Math.min(30, Math.max(0.1, z * factor));` — raw clamp, then `value: nz as NormalizedZoomValue`. Excalidraw always passes the new zoom through `getNormalizedZoom = clamp(round(zoom, 6), MIN_ZOOM, MAX_ZOOM)`, rounding to 6 decimal places.
expected: Zoom values are rounded to 6 dp before being stored (e.g. 0.1*3 stays 0.3, not 0.30000000000000004), and the displayed percentage is stable.
actual: Raw float multiplication is stored unrounded; repeated wheel/button zoom accumulates floating-point error in zoom.value, and the displayed % can flicker by 1 due to un-rounded values.

verdict.reasoning: Verified both cited locations. Port's zoomAt (draw-controller.svelte.ts:390-401) does `const nz = Math.min(30, Math.max(0.1, z * factor))` then stores `zoom: { value: nz as NormalizedZoomValue }` — raw clamped float, no rounding. Excalidraw (scene/normalize.ts:7) defines `getNormalizedZoom = clamp(round(zoom, 6), MIN_ZOOM, MAX_ZOOM)` and routes EVERY zoom mutation through it (verified: App.tsx:4329/12843, actionCanvas.tsx:148/189/230/334, restore.ts:1079). So the structural claim is correct: the port omits the round-to-6-dp normalization, and the same omission exists in #fitBounds (line 463) and the multiplicative wheel path (EditorPreview.svelte:180 uses Math.exp(...) as factor, an irrational multiplier that produces long mantissas every wheel tick). Repeated wheel zoom therefore accumulates un-rounded floating-point dust in zoom.value that Excalidraw would have rounded away.\n\nHowever, the cited *user-visible* symptom is overstated. The displayed percentage flicker is essentially a non-issue: both apps round at display time (port StatusBar.svelte:29 `Math.round(camera.zoom * 100)`; Excalidraw actionCanvas.tsx:250 `(zoom.value * 100).toFixed(0)`), and 6-dp-level dust (<1e-6, and even accumulated it stays far below 0.005) cannot flip the displayed integer percent. The real, but tiny, divergence is that the stored zoom.value differs from Excalidraw's by sub-1e-6 amounts after repeated operations, which feeds into scroll/render/hit-test math that divides by zoom. The magnitude is negligible (well below sub-pixel) and never produces a wrong result a user could perceive. It is a genuine fidelity gap in the stored value (so not benign-equivalent in the strict sense — the numbers genuinely diverge), but the practical impact is cosmetic at most.

title: Mouse-wheel zoom uses Math.exp(-deltaY*0.001) instead of Excalidraw's delta/100 + log10 amplification, and ignores MAX_STEP clamp
evidence: Our onwheel: `controller.zoomAt(Math.exp(-e.deltaY * 0.001), e.clientX, e.clientY)`. Excalidraw clamps delta to `MAX_STEP = ZOOM_STEP*100`, computes `newZoom = zoom - delta/100`, then adds `log10(max(1, zoom)) * -sign * min(1, absDelta/20)` to accelerate zoom past 100%, then `getNormalizedZoom(newZoom)`.
expected: Wheel zoom speed is linear in deltaY below 100% and accelerates (log10 term) above 100%; a single fast wheel tick is capped at MAX_STEP so zoom can't jump wildly.
actual: Wheel zoom is purely exponential in deltaY with a fixed 0.001 sensitivity, no MAX_STEP cap and no above-100% acceleration — zoom feel/speed differs from Excalidraw, and large trackpad deltas aren't clamped.

verdict.reasoning:
Both cited locations say exactly what the claim states. Ours (EditorPreview.svelte:177-184) calls `controller.zoomAt(Math.exp(-e.deltaY * 0.001), ...)`, and `zoomAt` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:390-401) does a purely MULTIPLICATIVE update: `nz = clamp(z * factor, 0.1, 30)`. So for a wheel event, `newZoom = oldZoom * exp(-deltaY*0.001)` with fixed 0.001 sensitivity, no delta clamp, no zoom-level-dependent acceleration.

Excalidraw (App.tsx:12819-12851) does an ADDITIVE update: clamps `delta` to `MAX_STEP = ZOOM_STEP*100 = 10` (ZOOM_STEP=0.1 confirmed in constants.ts), then `newZoom = zoom - delta/100`, then adds `log10(max(1,zoom)) * -sign * min(1, absDelta/20)` to accelerate zoom above 100%, then `getNormalizedZoom` clamps to [0.1, 30].

The behavioral divergences are genuine and user-visible:
1. Missing MAX_STEP clamp: a single large trackpad/mouse fling (e.g. deltaY=500) in ours gives factor exp(-0.5)≈0.607, dropping zoom to ~60% in one event (deltaY=1000 → 0.368). Excalidraw caps a single event's base step at delta=10 → ~0.1 zoom change. Ours jumps far more wildly on large deltas.
2. No above-100% acceleration: Excalidraw's log10 term makes zoom step grow with current zoom (at zoom=10 it adds ~1.0*amplification); ours has constant exponential rate regardless of zoom level.
3. Different curve shape (additive-linear vs multiplicative-exponential) even for small deltas → different zoom feel/speed.

This is the canvas-view domain (core interaction), not out-of-scope collab/AI/i18n. Both still clamp to [0.1,30] and center correctly on the cursor, so it is not a correctness/wrong-result bug — zoom remains functional. It is a meaningful behavioral/feel divergence, most concretely the missing large-delta clamp. Hence partial, not bug.

title: Wheel zoom anchors on the live event point but Excalidraw anchors on lastViewportPosition; minor, but Shift+wheel horizontal-pan semantics differ
evidence: Our non-zoom branch always does `controller.panBy(e.deltaX, e.deltaY)`. Excalidraw has a dedicated `event.shiftKey` branch that pans horizontally using `(deltaY || deltaX)` (Mac shift+wheel emits deltaY), distinct from the normal `scrollX -= deltaX, scrollY -= deltaY` branch.
expected: Shift+wheel scrolls horizontally even on trackpads/mice that report vertical deltaY while Shift is held (`scrollX -= (deltaY||deltaX)/zoom`).
actual: Shift+wheel just forwards deltaX/deltaY to panBy; on a mouse that reports only deltaY, Shift+wheel scrolls vertically instead of horizontally.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading both cited locations.

Excalidraw (App.tsx ~12853-12865) has three distinct wheel branches: (1) meta/ctrl zoom, (2) a dedicated `if (event.shiftKey)` branch doing `scrollX: scrollX - (deltaY || deltaX) / zoom.value` (comment: "on Mac, shift+wheel tends to result in deltaX"), and (3) the normal branch `scrollX -= deltaX/zoom, scrollY -= deltaY/zoom`. The shift branch deliberately routes BOTH deltaY and deltaX into horizontal scroll via `(deltaY || deltaX)`.

Ours (EditorPreview.svelte:177-184): the wheel handler has only two branches — ctrl/meta zoom, else `controller.panBy(e.deltaX, e.deltaY)`. There is no shiftKey branch. panBy (draw-controller.svelte.ts:381-387) does `scrollX -= viewportDx/zoom, scrollY -= viewportDy/zoom`, which exactly replicates Excalidraw's NORMAL branch only.

Realistic divergent action: a user on a standard mouse (reports only deltaY) holds Shift and scrolls. Excalidraw scrolls horizontally (`scrollX -= deltaY/zoom`); ours scrolls vertically (`panBy(0, deltaY)` → `scrollY -= deltaY/zoom`). This is a user-visible difference for a common, standard interaction (Shift+wheel = horizontal scroll). The cited code says exactly what the claim states.

Caveat: the claim's title also mentions "anchors on lastViewportPosition vs live event point" for zoom — that part is a benign anchoring nuance and somewhat of a red herring, but the substantive asserted divergence (missing Shift+wheel horizontal-pan semantics) is real and accurately cited.

title: zoom-to-fit / zoom-to-selection use a hard-coded 0.85 padding factor and skip roundToStep + the no-zoom-past-100% cap
evidence: #fitBounds: `zoom = clamp(min(width/w, height/h) * 0.85)`. Excalidraw's zoomValueToFitBoundsOnViewport multiplies by `clamp(viewportZoomFactor, 0.1, 1)` (factor defaults to 1, not 0.85), then `Math.min(adjustedZoomValue, 1)` (never zooms in past 100% for zoomToFit), then `roundToStep(adjustedZoomValue, ZOOM_STEP, 'floor')` to snap to a 0.1 grid.
expected: Zoom-to-fit fits content to exactly the viewport (factor 1), caps at 100%, and snaps the result down to the nearest 0.1 step.
actual: Our fit always leaves a 15% margin (0.85) and produces un-snapped zoom values, and for small content it would zoom in past 100% (our clamp upper bound is 30, with no Math.min(...,1) cap).

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed in code. Our zoom-to-fit/zoom-to-selection in src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts:454-467 (#fitBounds, called by zoomToFit() at 470-475 and zoomToSelection() at 477-483) computes: `zoom = Math.min(30, Math.max(0.1, Math.min(a.width/w, a.height/h) * 0.85))` and casts straight to NormalizedZoomValue with no normalization/rounding (the `as NormalizedZoomValue` cast is type-only; no runtime snap exists in the x/ path). This is the live Excalidraw-port surface, wired via EditorPreview.svelte and TitleBar.svelte to controller.zoomToFit().

Excalidraw (actionCanvas.tsx): actionZoomToFit (Shift+1) and actionZoomToFitSelection (Shift+3) both call zoomToFit with viewportZoomFactor undefined -> defaults to 1, NOT 0.85, so they fill the effective viewport exactly (minus getEditorUIOffsets chrome). The final zoom is `getNormalizedZoom(clamp(roundToStep(adjustedZoomValue, ZOOM_STEP=0.1, 'floor'), minZoom, maxZoom))` — i.e. floored to the nearest 0.1 step. Additionally, actionZoomToFit uses fitToViewport:false -> zoomValueToFitBoundsOnViewport applies `Math.min(adjustedZoomValue, 1)`, capping at 100%.

All three claimed divergences are real and produce different, user-visible zoom values:
(1) 0.85 vs 1.0 factor: ours always leaves a hard 15% margin; excalidraw fits to the viewport (minus UI offsets). Different result every time.
(2) Missing roundToStep snap: ours yields un-snapped values (e.g. 0.847 instead of 0.8). Different result.
(3) Missing Math.min(...,1) cap for zoom-to-fit: for small content (e.g. a 50x50 element in a 1400x900 viewport), excalidraw's actionZoomToFit -> 100%, ours -> ~1530%. Massive divergence.

One overstatement in the claim: it lumps zoom-to-*selection* with the 100% cap, but excalidraw's actionZoomToFitSelection uses fitToViewport:true and does NOT cap at 100% (intentionally zooms beyond 100%), so the cap divergence is specific to zoom-to-fit, not selection. The 0.85-factor and missing-roundToStep divergences apply to both. Net: a genuine behavioral bug, with the cap portion of the claim being accurate only for zoom-to-fit.

title: zoom-to-fit / zoom-to-selection / scroll-to-content ignore editor UI offsets (toolbar/panels), so content is mis-centered
evidence: #fitBounds and scrollToContent center on `width/2/zoom - cx` with no offsets. Excalidraw passes `canvasOffsets: app.getEditorUIOffsets()` into zoomToFit → centerScrollOn, which adds `offsets.left/2/zoom` and subtracts `offsets.right` so content is centered in the *visible* canvas area (excluding the left properties panel / right panels).
expected: Fit/scroll centers content in the region not covered by the floating UI panels, accounting for left/right/top/bottom offsets.
actual: Content is centered in the full viewport, so with the left properties panel open the content sits partly under the panel rather than centered in the free space.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified both cited locations. Excalidraw's actionCanvas.tsx passes `canvasOffsets: app.getEditorUIOffsets()` into `zoomToFit`/`zoomToFitBounds` (lines 399-409, 425-435, 451-459). `getEditorUIOffsets` (App.tsx:4706-4743) measures the left properties panel (`.App-menu__left`) right-edge, the right sidebar (`.sidebar`) left-edge, and the toolbar bottom, plus 16px padding. `zoomToFitBounds` (actionCanvas.tsx:277-357) computes zoom against `effectiveCanvasWidth/Height = width - left - right` etc, and feeds the offsets into `centerScrollOn` (scroll.ts:31-58), which does `scrollX = (width - right)/2/zoom - x; scrollX += left/2/zoom` (and the Y analog), so content is centered in the panel-free region.

Our port's `#fitBounds` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:454-467) and `scrollToContent` (485-499) center purely on `a.width/2/zoom - cx` / `a.height/2/zoom - cy` with no offset term. There is no offsets concept wired in (getEditorUIOffsets is only a stubbed `unknown` in the excalidraw type-compat shim, src/lib/excalidraw/components/App.ts:70; nothing in src/lib/x uses it).
