The architecture matches Excalidraw, which makes the divergence real rather than moot: the canvas is full-bleed (`.canvas-full { position:absolute; inset:0 }`, +page.svelte:713-716) and the LeftPanel/RightPanel are floating Islands absolutely positioned over it (`.panel-dock { position:absolute }`, web mode insets at lines 871-884). Canvas.svelte sizes appState width/height from the full-bleed host rect (lines 103-106), so `appState.width` is the whole window, not the panel-excluded region. Therefore, with the left layers panel and/or right inspector open, pressing zoom-to-fit (Shift+1) / zoom-to-selection / scroll-to-content centers content on the full viewport, leaving it shifted toward/under the panels instead of optically centered in the visible free space, exactly as claimed. This is a genuine runtime behavioral difference for a realistic user action.

Severity: the content still fully fits and stays on-screen (our fit even applies a 0.85 padding factor), and no data is wrong — only the centering is optically off when panels are open. It is a visible fidelity miss, not a functional break, so cosmetic.

title: reset-zoom zeroes scroll to (0,0) instead of preserving the viewport-center scene point
evidence: resetView(): sets `zoom:1, scrollX:0, scrollY:0`. Excalidraw's actionResetZoom calls `getStateForZoom({ viewportX: width/2+offsetLeft, viewportY: height/2+offsetTop, nextZoom: getNormalizedZoom(1) }, appState)`, which keeps the scene point currently under the viewport center fixed while changing zoom to 1.
expected: Reset zoom (the footer % button) returns to 100% while keeping whatever scene point was centered in the viewport centered — the canvas does not jump.
actual: Reset view snaps zoom to 100% AND teleports scroll to origin (0,0), jumping the camera back to the world origin instead of staying on the current content.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed real divergence. Our resetView() (draw-controller.svelte.ts:445-451) hard-sets `scrollX: 0, scrollY: 0` along with `zoom: 1`. Excalidraw's actionResetZoom (actionCanvas.tsx:222-237) instead spreads `getStateForZoom({ viewportX: width/2 + offsetLeft, viewportY: height/2 + offsetTop, nextZoom: getNormalizedZoom(1) }, appState)`. Reading scene/zoom.ts:14-35, getStateForZoom computes new scrollX/scrollY that keep the scene point under the viewport center anchored while changing zoom — it never zeroes scroll. Both are wired to the same affordance: Excalidraw's footer % button (actionResetZoom PanelComponent) and our footer .zoom-label button at EditorPreview.svelte:854 both call this reset. Realistic action: pan to content far from origin, click the footer % button. Excalidraw keeps the centered content fixed (and when already at zoom=1, getStateForZoom's scroll offsets cancel, leaving scroll untouched); ours teleports the camera to world origin (0,0), making the user's content jump out of view. The cited code says exactly what the claim states and produces a visibly different camera position for the same user gesture.

title: No keyboard shortcuts for zoom in/out/reset (Ctrl++ / Ctrl+- / Ctrl+0) or zoom-to-fit/selection (Shift+1/2/3)
evidence: onkeydown handles many shortcuts but none for zoom. Excalidraw registers keyTests: zoomIn (`(EQUAL||NUM_ADD) && (CtrlOrCmd||shift)`), zoomOut (`(MINUS||NUM_SUBTRACT) && (CtrlOrCmd||shift)`), resetZoom (`(ZERO||NUM_ZERO) && (CtrlOrCmd||shift)`), zoomToFit (`Shift+1`), zoomToFitSelectionInViewport (`Shift+2`), zoomToFitSelection (`Shift+3`).
expected: Ctrl/Cmd+= zooms in, Ctrl/Cmd+- zooms out, Ctrl/Cmd+0 resets zoom, Shift+1 fits all, Shift+2/3 fit selection.
actual: None of these keyboard shortcuts are wired — zoom is only reachable via footer buttons / command palette.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified both cited locations. Excalidraw (actionCanvas.tsx) registers zoom keyTests exactly as claimed: actionZoomIn `(EQUAL||NUM_ADD) && (CtrlOrCmd||shift)` (170-172), actionZoomOut `(MINUS||NUM_SUBTRACT) && (CtrlOrCmd||shift)` (211-213), actionResetZoom `(ZERO||NUM_ZERO) && (CtrlOrCmd||shift)` (254-256), actionZoomToFit `ONE && shift` (461-465), actionZoomToFitSelectionInViewport `TWO && shift` (413-417), actionZoomToFitSelection `THREE && shift` (438-442).

The cited port file src/lib/x/EditorPreview.svelte is the Excalidraw-port render path, mounted at route /x (src/routes/x/+page.svelte imports it). Its onkeydown handler (lines 317-416) is the sole window keydown listener registered via `<svelte:window {onkeydown} {onkeyup} />` (line 539). I read the entire handler: it covers delete, escape, duplicate, copy/paste styles, copy/cut/paste, z-order, undo/redo, command palette, save/open, grid, select-all, group/ungroup, flip, help, and tool keys — but contains ZERO zoom branches (no Equal/Minus/Digit0, no Shift+1/2/3). The other window-keydown handlers in the x/ tree (MainMenu, ContextMenu, dialogs) only handle Escape/menu nav, not zoom. The draw-controller has no zoomIn/zoomOut/resetZoom methods at all (only a `zoom` getter, zoomToFit, zoomToSelection), and zoomToFit/zoomToSelection are reachable only via footer buttons (TitleBar/Canvas) and the command palette — never keyboard.

So in the /x Excalidraw-port app, pressing Ctrl/Cmd+=, Ctrl/Cmd+-, Ctrl/Cmd+0, or Shift+1/2/3 produces no zoom action, whereas Excalidraw zooms in/out/resets/fits. This is a genuine user-visible divergence.

Caveat for accuracy: a SEPARATE legacy LayoutForge editor at route / (src/routes/+page.svelte, using the `editor` singleton) DOES implement all these shortcuts (lines 416-497). But that is a different app/code path, not the cited one, and not the Excalidraw port. The cited port path genuinely lacks the shortcuts.

title: View-mode and zen-mode toggles have no keyboard shortcuts (Alt+R / Alt+Z) and no phone-form-factor / appProps gating
evidence: toggleViewMode/toggleZenMode just flip the boolean; no keyTest is wired in onkeydown. Excalidraw binds viewMode to `!CtrlOrCmd && altKey && code==='R'` and zenMode to `!CtrlOrCmd && altKey && code==='Z'`.
expected: Alt+R toggles view mode and Alt+Z toggles zen mode.
actual: Neither Alt+R nor Alt+Z does anything; these modes are only reachable from the main menu / command palette.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified both cited locations. Excalidraw binds the shortcuts via keyTest: actionToggleViewMode.tsx:31-32 → `!event[KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD] && event.altKey && event.code === CODES.R` (Alt+R), and actionToggleZenMode.tsx:34-35 → same with `CODES.Z` (Alt+Z). Both actions are registered, so these keys toggle the modes globally in Excalidraw.

In the port, draw-controller.svelte.ts:1058-1067 `toggleViewMode`/`toggleZenMode` merely flip the appState boolean with no key dispatch. The canvas keydown handler that drives all global shortcuts is `onkeydown` in EditorPreview.svelte:317-416 (bound via `<svelte:window {onkeydown} />` at line 539). I read the full if/else-if chain: it handles Backspace/Delete, Escape, Cmd+D, Cmd+Alt+C/V, Cmd+C/X/V, Cmd+[/], Cmd+Z, Cmd+/, Cmd+S/O, Cmd+', Cmd+A, Cmd+G, Shift+H/V, '?', and TOOL_KEYS — but there is NO branch for `altKey && (e.key==='r'/'z')`. grep across src/ for altKey+R/Z keyboard handling found nothing. The only other window keydown handlers (MainMenu, ContextMenu, dialogs) early-return unless the dialog is open and only intercept Escape. So with no dialog open, pressing Alt+R or Alt+Z does nothing in the port.

The modes are reachable only via command palette (EditorPreview.svelte:229-230) and the menu (304-305). This is a genuine, user-visible behavioral divergence: a realistic user pressing Alt+R / Alt+Z in Excalidraw toggles view/zen mode; in the port nothing happens.

Note: the claim's secondary "appProps / phone-form-factor gating" point refers to Excalidraw's `predicate` (action availability when a host app forces the mode or on a phone). That is out-of-scope for this single-user desktop Tauri app and is benign on its own. But the missing keyboard shortcuts are a real bug, so the claim stands as real.

title: deleteSelected never calls fixBindingsAfterDeletion — arrows keep dangling bindings to deleted shapes
evidence: Ours: `this.#elements = this.#elements.filter((e) => !ids.has(e.id)); this.#select(null); syncInvalidIndices(...); replaceAllElements(...)`. No binding fix-up. Excalidraw runs `fixBindingsAfterDeletion(nextElements, nextElements.filter((el) => el.isDeleted))` after deletion, and the inline elbow-arrow `startBinding/endBinding` nulling loop (lines 97-110). `fixBindingsAfterDeletion` exists in our repo (src/lib/element/binding.ts:2061) but is never invoked from deleteSelected.
expected: After deletion, arrows bound to the deleted element have their startBinding/endBinding cleared and boundElements references on survivors are cleaned up.
actual: Bound arrows retain startBinding/endBinding pointing at now-removed element ids; survivor elements keep stale boundElements entries — broken binding state that can crash drag/resize of the orphaned arrow.

verdict.reasoning: Verified at both cited locations. Excalidraw's actionDeleteSelected (actionDeleteSelected.tsx:278-281) explicitly calls `fixBindingsAfterDeletion(nextElements, nextElements.filter(el => el.isDeleted))` plus an inline elbow-arrow startBinding/endBinding nulling loop (lines 97-110). Our deleteSelected (draw-controller.svelte.ts:1254-1258) hard-deletes via `this.#elements = this.#elements.filter((e) => !ids.has(e.id))` then `#select(null)`, `syncInvalidIndices`, `replaceAllElements`, `#commit` — with no binding fix-up. grep confirms `fixBindingsAfterDeletion` is defined in our repo (binding.ts:2061) but never called anywhere in src/. I read both unbindAffected impls: BindableElement.unbindAffected (line 2333) sets the surviving arrow's startBinding/endBinding to null; BoundElement.unbindAffected (line 2193) strips the deleted id from survivors' boundElements. Neither runs in our port. So this is a genuine state divergence: after deleting a bound shape, our surviving arrows keep startBinding/endBinding pointing at the removed element id and survivor bindables keep stale boundElements entries, whereas Excalidraw clears both. This stale state is persisted to the saved .lfdoc and affects subsequent binding-dependent operations and serialization. HOWEVER, the claim's "can crash drag/resize of the orphaned arrow" is overstated: I checked the binding read paths (getGlobalFixedPoints:2431-2440, updateBoundElements:1140-1148) — all use elementsMap.get(id) (returns undefined for a removed element) and null-check defensively (`startElement && arrow.startBinding`, `bindableElement && isBindableElement(...)`), so the orphaned arrow falls back to its own points rather than crashing. The divergence is real and user-affecting (binding state never cleaned, diverges from Excalidraw, persisted), but it is a correctness/state-cleanup bug, not a crash.

title: deleteSelected does a hard filter instead of isDeleted flag, and ignores frames/bound-text/elbow-arrow handling
evidence: Ours removes elements outright: `this.#elements.filter((e) => !ids.has(e.id))`. Excalidraw's deleteSelectedElements: (a) when a frame is selected, removes children from the frame and SELECTS them rather than deleting (lines 84-122), (b) deletes the bound text when its container is deleted (`isBoundToContainer(el) && selectedElementIds[el.containerId]`, lines 124-126), (c) re-selects the next eligible element in the editing group / supergroup (lines 130-170).
expected: Deleting a frame keeps and selects its children; deleting a container deletes its bound text; deleting inside an editing group re-selects a sibling.
actual: Deleting a frame deletes only the frame element (children orphaned, not re-selected); a deleted container may leave its bound text orphaned; editing-group re-selection never happens.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading both cited locations. Our deleteSelected (draw-controller.svelte.ts:1254) is `this.#elements = this.#elements.filter((e) => !ids.has(e.id))` followed by `this.#select(null)`. Excalidraw's deleteSelectedElements (actionDeleteSelected.tsx:39-187) implements four behaviors ours lacks, each tied to features that ARE in scope in this port:

(1) Frame deletion (lines 84-122): when a frame is selected, its children are NOT deleted — they get `newElementWith(el, { frameId: null })` and are re-selected via `selectedElementIds[el.id] = true`. Ours puts only the frame's own id in `ids`, so the filter removes the frame and leaves children with a dangling `frameId`, and they are NOT re-selected (we call `#select(null)`). Frames and getFrameChildren are imported/used in the port (draw-controller lines 16,25,1852-1853), so this is a live, user-visible divergence: deleting a frame in ours orphans the children without re-selecting them.

(2) Bound-text orphaning (lines 124-126): Excalidraw deletes a bound text element when its container is in selectedElementIds (`isBoundToContainer(el) && appState.selectedElementIds[el.containerId]`). In ours, selectedIds (a SvelteSet, line 205) holds only directly-selected ids; a container's bound text is never selected on its own, so `ids.has(boundText.id)` is false and the text SURVIVES the filter. Result: deleting a labeled rectangle leaves its label floating on the canvas with a containerId pointing at a removed element. Bound-text containers are fully supported in the port (containerId across element/*.ts). Clear user-visible bug.

(3) Editing-group re-selection (lines 130-170): Excalidraw re-selects the next eligible sibling in the editing group/supergroup. Ours unconditionally calls `#select(null)`. editingGroupId/groups are in scope (draw-controller lines 985,1025,1035). Selection differs after deleting inside an edited group.

(4) Elbow-arrow unbinding + fixBindingsAfterDeletion (lines 97-111, 278-281): absent from our deleteSelected (no fixBindingsAfterDeletion call; isElbowArrow only used at line 2550 in unrelated code). Deleting an element bound to an elbow arrow leaves a stale binding. Compounded by the hard-filter: because we physically remove elements instead of flagging isDeleted, even an Excalidraw-style fixBindingsAfterDeletion pass (which iterates `isDeleted` elements) could not find them.

The hard-filter-vs-isDeleted point in isolation is benign (deleted elements aren't rendered either way), but it is the root cause that makes the binding/bound-text/frame handling impossible. The aggregate produces multiple realistic wrong results (orphaned labels, orphaned frame children, lost group selection, stale arrow bindings).

title: Delete key fires regardless of Ctrl/Cmd modifier
evidence: Ours: `if (e.key === 'Backspace' || e.key === 'Delete') { controller.deleteSelected(); ... }` with no modifier guard. Excalidraw keyTest: `(event.key === KEYS.BACKSPACE || event.key === KEYS.DELETE) && !event[KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD]`.
expected: Cmd/Ctrl+Backspace does NOT delete the selection.
actual: Cmd+Backspace (a common macOS 'delete word/line' chord) deletes the selected elements.

verdict.reasoning:
Both cited locations confirm the claim. Our handler at src/lib/x/EditorPreview.svelte:328 reads `if (e.key === 'Backspace' || e.key === 'Delete') { controller.deleteSelected(); e.preventDefault(); }` with no modifier guard. The preceding branches (311-327) only handle the Space key, so nothing short-circuits a Cmd/Ctrl+Backspace press — it falls into this branch and deletes the selection.

Excalidraw's actionDeleteSelected.tsx:305-307 keyTest is `(event.key === KEYS.BACKSPACE || event.key === KEYS.DELETE) && !event[KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD]`, explicitly excluding the Ctrl/Cmd modifier. KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD resolves to `metaKey` on Darwin (keys.ts:39). Crucially, excalidraw also has a competing binding at App.tsx:5311-5316: when `event[KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD] && (BACKSPACE || DELETE)`, it opens the "clearCanvas" confirm dialog instead. So the modifier exclusion in the delete keyTest is deliberate — Cmd+Backspace is reserved for clear-canvas, NOT delete.

Net behavior: on macOS, Cmd+Backspace in excalidraw does not delete the selection (it offers clear-canvas), whereas in our port it deletes the selected elements. This is a genuine, user-visible runtime divergence triggered by a common macOS chord. Not collab/AI/i18n, not a misread. Severity is a behavioral bug rather than partial/cosmetic: a realistic keypress produces accidental destructive deletion that excalidraw does not.

title: duplicateSelected uses a fresh group-id map per element, breaking grouped duplicates
evidence: Ours calls `duplicateElement(null, new Map(), orig, true)` inside `originals.map(...)`, so each element gets its OWN empty `new Map()` group-id map. duplicateElement (src/lib/element/duplicate.ts:80-89) remaps each original groupId to a fresh randomId via that map; with a per-element map, two originals sharing groupId 'G' get DIFFERENT new group ids. Excalidraw routes the whole selection through `duplicateElements({type:'in-place', ...})` which shares one groupIdMap across the batch (and rewires bindings & frame ids).
expected: Duplicating a group yields copies that are themselves a single new group (and bound arrows in the copy bind to the duplicated targets).
actual: Duplicated members of the same original group land in separate singleton groups (group is lost); copied arrows stay bound to the ORIGINAL elements instead of the duplicates.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed genuine divergence. Our `duplicateSelected()` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:1678-1683) iterates `originals.map((orig) => ... duplicateElement(null, new Map(), orig, true))`, giving each element its OWN empty `new Map()` group-id map. `duplicateElement` (duplicate.ts:80-89) remaps group ids via `getNewGroupIdsForDuplication` -> mapper, which for each groupId does `if (!groupIdMapForOperation.has(groupId)) groupIdMapForOperation.set(groupId, randomId())`. With a per-element map, two originals that share group 'G' each start with an empty map and therefore mint a DIFFERENT new random group id -> the duplicated group is broken into separate singleton groups. Verified getNewGroupIdsForDuplication (groups.ts:390-406) indeed delegates per-groupId to the mapper, so the shared-vs-fresh map distinction is load-bearing.

Additionally the controller's hand-rolled loop never runs `fixDuplicatedBindingsAfterDuplication` or `bindElementsToFramesAfterDuplication`, which Excalidraw's batch `duplicateElements` performs (duplicate.ts:388-398). So copied arrows retain startBinding/endBinding pointing at the ORIGINAL element ids rather than the duplicates, and frame children keep original frameId.

Excalidraw's actionDuplicateSelection.tsx:63-83 routes the whole selection through `duplicateElements({type:'in-place', ..., randomizeSeed:true, overrides:...})`, which uses a SINGLE shared `groupIdMap` (duplicate.ts:159) across the batch and applies binding + frame rewiring. The full batch `duplicateElements` already exists in our port (duplicate.ts:93-422) but is simply not wired into duplicateSelected(). Realistic user actions affected: Cmd/Ctrl+D (or alt-drag-equivalent duplicate) on a grouped selection -> group lost; duplicating an arrow bound between two duplicated shapes -> copy stays bound to originals. Both are user-visible wrong results.

title: selectAll selects locked elements and bound text labels
evidence: Ours: `selectAll() { this.#setSelection(this.scene.elements.map((e) => e.id)); }` — every non-deleted element. Excalidraw filters: `!element.isDeleted && !(isTextElement(element) && element.containerId) && !element.locked`. It also sets `selectedLinearElement` when exactly one linear element is selected.
expected: Cmd+A selects unlocked, non-bound-text elements only; locked elements stay unselected; a single linear selection enters point-edit-capable state.
actual: Cmd+A selects locked elements (which are otherwise unselectable) and selects bound text labels independently of their containers; never sets selectedLinearElement.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified at both cited locations. Ours (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts:888-890): `selectAll() { this.#setSelection(this.scene.elements.map((e) => e.id)); }` — maps EVERY non-deleted element id with no filtering. `#setSelection` (line 1808-1816) applies the ids verbatim. Excalidraw (actionSelectAll.ts:28-38) filters out `element.locked` and bound text (`isTextElement(element) && element.containerId`), and at lines 52-57 sets `selectedLinearElement` when exactly one linear element is selected.

LOCKED ELEMENTS — REAL BUG: The port treats locked elements as otherwise unselectable: hit-test skips them (line 1793-1794 `if (element.locked) continue; // locked elements are not selectable`), and `lockSelected` deselects after locking (line 941). But selectAll bypasses all of that, dumping locked ids straight into `selectedIds`. `selectedElements` (1706-1710) returns them with no locked filter, and `deleteSelected` (1250-1258) removes every id in `selectedIds` with no locked check. So the realistic chain Cmd+A → Delete deletes locked elements in our port, whereas Excalidraw excludes them from selectAll so they survive. Cmd+A is wired to selectAll (src/routes/+page.svelte:319). This is a clear, user-visible contradiction of the lock feature.

BOUND TEXT: The element model fully supports bound text (containerId, getBoundTextElement, redrawTextBoundingBox are used in element/binding.ts and the controller). selectAll would add bound-text label ids independently of their containers, which Excalidraw filters out. Reachability of bound text in this controller is less clear (no double-click-into-container creation path found in draw-controller), so this sub-claim is real-but-secondary.

selectedLinearElement: Confirmed ours never sets it in selectAll; Excalidraw sets it for a single selected linear element. A real but minor UX gap.

The cited code says exactly what the claim states; evidence quotes are exact. The primary divergence (selecting + then deleting/moving/transforming locked elements) is a genuine behavioral bug.

title: Bring-to-front / send-to-back use Cmd+Shift+[ ] on macOS; Excalidraw uses Cmd+Alt+[ ] on Darwin
evidence: Ours: `(meta||ctrl) && e.key === ']'` then `if (e.shiftKey) bringToFront() else bringForward()` (and same for '['). Excalidraw sendToBack/bringToFront keyTest: `isDarwin ? CTRL_OR_CMD && altKey && BRACKET : CTRL_OR_CMD && shiftKey && BRACKET`. This is a macOS Tauri app (isDarwin true), so the reference chord is Cmd+Alt+[ / Cmd+Alt+].
expected: On macOS, Cmd+Alt+[ = send to back, Cmd+Alt+] = bring to front; Cmd+Shift+[ ] do nothing special.
actual: Cmd+Alt+[ / Cmd+Alt+] do nothing (Alt branch not handled); we use Cmd+Shift+[ ] instead, conflicting with muscle memory and any OS-level Cmd+Alt chord.

verdict.reasoning:
Both cited locations read as claimed and the divergence is genuine on the target macOS (Tauri, isDarwin=true) platform.

Excalidraw (actionZindex.tsx): sendBackward = CtrlOrCmd + !shift + BRACKET_LEFT (Cmd+[), bringForward = CtrlOrCmd + !shift + BRACKET_RIGHT (Cmd+]). For the "all the way" actions, on Darwin: sendToBack keyTest (lines 96-100) = CTRL_OR_CMD && altKey && BRACKET_LEFT → Cmd+Alt+[; bringToFront keyTest (lines 134-138) = CTRL_OR_CMD && altKey && BRACKET_RIGHT → Cmd+Alt+]. On Darwin, Cmd+Shift+[ / Cmd+Shift+] match nothing (sendToBack/bringToFront require altKey, and sendBackward/bringForward require !shiftKey).

Ours (EditorPreview.svelte:358-371): `(meta||ctrl) && e.key === ']'` then `if (e.shiftKey) bringToFront() else bringForward()`, and the mirror for '['. So we map bringToFront/sendToBack to Cmd+Shift+]/Cmd+Shift+[, and never test altKey.

Resulting divergence on macOS: (1) Cmd+Shift+] / Cmd+Shift+[ trigger bringToFront/sendToBack in our port, but do nothing in Excalidraw. (2) Cmd+Alt+] / Cmd+Alt+[ trigger bringToFront/sendToBack in Excalidraw, but in our port do not — additionally, because we test e.key (not event.code), Option+] on a US layout yields a different e.key character, so Cmd+Alt+] won't even match the ']' branch and does nothing. Either way the two implementations produce different results for the same physical keystrokes on the same OS. Single-step Cmd+[/Cmd+] (no modifier) agree.

This is a user-visible keyboard-shortcut mismatch for a core z-ordering operation on the only platform the app targets. Not collab/AI/i18n; not a misread.

title: Lock toggle keyboard shortcut (Cmd+Shift+L) is unbound
evidence: The keydown handler has no branch for `key==='l' && CTRL_OR_CMD && shiftKey`. lockSelected() exists (draw-controller line 942) but is only reachable via command palette / context menu (EditorPreview line 280). Excalidraw binds `event.key.toLowerCase()===KEYS.L && CTRL_OR_CMD && shiftKey`.
expected: Cmd+Shift+L toggles lock on the selection.
actual: Cmd+Shift+L does nothing.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed at both cited locations. In our port, EditorPreview.svelte (the live editor mounted at the /x route via src/routes/x/+page.svelte; its onkeydown is the global handler wired through `<svelte:window {onkeydown}>` at line 539), the keydown function (lines 317-416) walks every else-if branch and has NO branch matching `(e.metaKey||e.ctrlKey) && e.shiftKey && e.key==='l'`. The only `l` branch is the tool shortcut at lines 412-413, but it is gated by `!e.metaKey && !e.ctrlKey`, so Cmd+Shift+L falls through to the end and does nothing. `controller.lockSelected()` exists (draw-controller.svelte.ts:942) but is only wired to the context menu 'Lock' item (EditorPreview line 280) — never to any keyboard shortcut. In excalidraw-master (actionElementLock.ts:143-153) the action's keyTest binds `event.key.toLowerCase()===KEYS.L && event[CTRL_OR_CMD] && event.shiftKey && selectedElements.length>0`, and KEYS.L is 'l' (common/src/keys.ts:61), toggling lock on the selection. Realistic action: select element(s), press Cmd+Shift+L — Excalidraw locks/toggles the selection, our port does nothing. Genuine user-visible behavioral divergence for an in-scope local editing feature (lock is not collab/AI/i18n).

title: Align keyboard shortcuts (Cmd+Shift+Arrow) are unbound
evidence: No keydown branch handles ArrowUp/Down/Left/Right. Excalidraw binds alignTop=Cmd+Shift+ArrowUp, alignBottom=Cmd+Shift+ArrowDown, alignLeft=Cmd+Shift+ArrowLeft, alignRight=Cmd+Shift+ArrowRight. alignSelected() exists in the controller but is only reachable via palette/context menu.
expected: Cmd+Shift+Arrow aligns the multi-selection in that direction.
actual: Cmd+Shift+Arrow keys do nothing.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified both cited locations. Excalidraw side (actionAlign.tsx:96-97,130-131,164-165,198-199): each align action registers a keyTest of `event[KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD] && event.shiftKey && event.key === KEYS.ARROW_{UP,DOWN,LEFT,RIGHT}`, gated by alignActionsPredicate which requires >1 selected group (and no frames). Registered actions with keyTest are dispatched globally on keydown, so with a multi-selection, Cmd+Shift+Arrow aligns the selection top/bottom/left/right.

Our side (EditorPreview.svelte onkeydown, lines 317-416): I read the entire handler. There is no branch handling ArrowUp/ArrowDown/ArrowLeft/ArrowRight at all — the if/else-if chain covers Backspace/Delete, Escape, Cmd+D, style copy/paste, clipboard, z-order brackets, undo/redo, palette, save/open, grid, select-all, group/ungroup, flip, '?', and tool keys, then ends. A grep across src/lib confirms no arrow-key align dispatch exists; arrow keys are only handled inside CommandPalette/FileMenu list navigation. controller.alignSelected(position, axis) exists (draw-controller.svelte.ts:914) but is wired only to the command palette (EditorPreview.svelte:218-221) and context menu (271-276) — never to a keyboard shortcut.

So for the realistic action 'select 2+ elements, press Cmd+Shift+ArrowUp', Excalidraw aligns the selection while our port does nothing. Genuine user-visible divergence, core editor functionality (not collab/AI/i18n).

title: Distribute keyboard shortcuts (Alt+H / Alt+V) are unbound
evidence: No keydown branch handles `altKey && code==='KeyH'/'KeyV'`. Excalidraw: distributeHorizontally = `!CTRL_OR_CMD && altKey && code===CODES.H`, distributeVertically = `!CTRL_OR_CMD && altKey && code===CODES.V`. Our only Alt handling is Cmd+Alt+C/V style-copy (lines 340-345).
expected: Alt+H distributes horizontally, Alt+V distributes vertically.
actual: Alt+H / Alt+V do nothing.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified both cited locations. Excalidraw's actionDistribute.tsx:87-88 and 118-119 bind distributeHorizontally to `!CTRL_OR_CMD && altKey && code===CODES.H` (Alt+H) and distributeVertically to `!CTRL_OR_CMD && altKey && code===CODES.V` (Alt+V) — confirmed exactly as claimed.

In the cited file src/lib/x/EditorPreview.svelte, the onkeydown handler (lines 317-416) is a complete if/else-if chain bound via `<svelte:window {onkeydown}>` (line 539). I read the entire chain: the only Alt handling is `(metaKey||ctrlKey) && altKey && c/v` for copy/paste styles (lines 340-345). There is NO branch for `altKey && code==='KeyH'/'KeyV'` to distribute. Worse, the bare-letter tool block at line 412 requires `!e.altKey`, so Alt+H/Alt+V fall through and do nothing in this editor. The distribute capability DOES exist (controller.distributeSelected('x'/'y'), draw-controller.svelte.ts:924-930) and is reachable from the command palette (EditorPreview.svelte:222-223) and context menu (277-278), but the keyboard shortcut is genuinely unbound here.

Important nuance the claim glosses over: there are TWO editors in this transitional repo. The main `/` route (src/routes/+page.svelte:466-475) is the legacy LayoutForge editor and DOES correctly bind Alt+H/Alt+V to commands.distribute('x'/'y') with a comment citing actionDistribute.tsx:87,118. The `/x` route renders EditorPreview.svelte, described in src/routes/x/+page.svelte as the "isolated dev route for the Excalidraw-port render path." Since revo-draw is explicitly being built as the faithful Excalidraw port, EditorPreview.svelte IS the parity surface under review, and it omits the shortcut. So for a user in the Excalidraw-port editor, pressing Alt+H/Alt+V with a multi-element selection produces no result, whereas real Excalidraw distributes. This is a real, user-visible behavioral divergence in the cited file.

Severity is partial rather than bug because the underlying distribute operation is fully implemented and accessible via two other UI paths (command palette + context menu); only the keyboard binding is missing.

title: alignSelected guards on element count (>=2) instead of group count (>1)
evidence: Ours: `if (sel.length < 2) return;`. Excalidraw alignActionsPredicate requires `getSelectedElementsByGroup(...).length > 1` and `!some(isFrameLikeElement)`. Selecting 2 elements that are in the same group is 1 unit for Excalidraw (align disabled / no-op), but our guard lets it through; align of a single group is a no-op but the menu/state still reports it as actionable.
expected: Align needs more than 1 independent unit (group) and no frames in selection.
actual: Align runs whenever ≥2 raw elements are selected, including a single group (no-op) and selections containing frames.

verdict.reasoning:
Both cited locations say what the claim states. Our port `draw-controller.svelte.ts:914-922` (`alignSelected`) guards only on `if (sel.length < 2) return;` then calls the faithful `alignElements(...)`. Excalidraw's `alignActionsPredicate` (actionAlign.tsx:39-53) gates the action on TWO conditions our guard omits: `getSelectedElementsByGroup(...).length > 1` (more than one independent unit) AND `!selectedElements.some(isFrameLikeElement)` (no frames).

Two divergences result, and the second is a real wrong-result bug:

1. Single-group case (cosmetic): Select 2+ elements all in the same group → Excalidraw's predicate is `1 > 1` = false, action disabled. Our guard (length >= 2) lets it through. But our port's `alignElements` (src/lib/element/align.ts) is a faithful copy that also groups via `getSelectedElementsByGroup`, so a single group yields one group whose bbox equals the selection bbox → translation 0 → no element moves. So clicking does nothing in both; only the UI actionable-state differs. Cosmetic.

2. Frame-inclusion case (genuine bug): Our port HAS frame elements (`type: "frame"`, `ExcalidrawFrameLikeElement`, `isFrameLikeElement` used across element/*) and they are selectable. Excalidraw deliberately disables align when any selected element is frame-like (the `!some(isFrameLikeElement)` clause, with the TODO comment). Our `alignSelected` has no frame check, so selecting a frame + another element and invoking align actually computes nonzero translations and moves elements. The path is reachable at runtime: EditorPreview.svelte:218-221 wires command-palette "Align left/right/top/bottom" directly to `controller.alignSelected`, with no predicate. RightPanel also gates align solely on `sel.length >= 2`. This is a user-visible behavioral divergence (elements/frames move when Excalidraw would do nothing), not a no-op or equivalence.

Not out-of-scope (frames and groups are ported and live). Reviewer did not misread. Severity: bug, driven by the frame case; the single-group sub-claim alone would only be cosmetic.

title: flipSelected omits the all-bound-arrows arrowhead-swap branch and the post-flip recentering
evidence: Ours calls resizeMultipleElements(...) with flipByX/Y then triggerUpdate+commit. Excalidraw flipElements: (1) if every selected element is a bound arrow, it instead swaps startArrowhead<->endArrowhead and returns (lines 116-129); (2) after resize it calls bindOrUnbindBindingElements (line 152); (3) it recenters the group back to the original midX/midY via diffX/diffY mutation (lines 158-193) so repeated flips don't drift. Ours does none of these.
expected: Flipping a selection of bound arrows swaps arrowheads; flipping any selection rebinds arrows and snaps the group center back so repeated flips don't accumulate offset.
actual: Flipping bound arrows resizes them (no arrowhead swap); arrows are not rebound; the selection can drift across the canvas on repeated flips.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified both cited locations. Ours (src/lib/x/draw-controller.svelte.ts:893-911) flipSelected: builds an originals map of all elements, calls resizeMultipleElements with flipByX/flipByY + shouldResizeFromCenter + shouldMaintainAspectRatio, then triggerUpdate + commit. That is the ENTIRE implementation.

Excalidraw flipElements (actionFlip.ts:110-196) does three things ours omits:
(1) lines 116-129: if every selected element is a bound arrow (isArrowElement && (startBinding||endBinding)), it returns early with each arrow having startArrowhead/endArrowhead swapped via newElementWith — NO resize at all. Ours unconditionally resizes.
(2) line 152: after resize, bindOrUnbindBindingElements(selectedElements.filter(isArrowElement), ...) re-binds arrows. Ours never calls this.
(3) lines 131,158-193: captures getCommonBoundingBox midX/midY before resize, recomputes newMidX/newMidY after, and mutates every element (and elbow arrows) by diffX=midX-newMidX / diffY=midY-newMidY to snap the group center back. The inline comment explicitly states this prevents repeated flips from accumulating offset. Ours has no recentering, so the committed positions are the raw post-resize ones.

These are genuine behavioral divergences, not benign/equivalent:
- Flipping a selection consisting solely of bound arrows: excalidraw swaps arrowheads (a directional reversal); ours mirror-resizes the arrow geometry. Different visible result.
- Repeated flips of bound arrows drift across the canvas in ours because the group center is not snapped back.
- Arrows aren't re-bound after flip in ours.

This is not out-of-scope infrastructure: the port HAS all required primitives — getCommonBoundingBox is exported (src/lib/element/bounds.ts:1158), bindOrUnbindBindingElements is exported (src/lib/element/binding.ts:999), binding fields startBinding/endBinding exist (src/lib/element/binding.ts), and isArrowElement/isElbowArrow are already imported in the controller (lines 22-23). So all three branches are implementable and simply absent. Confirmed real bug.

title: groupSelected does not reorder z-indices to make the new group contiguous, nor update selection state
evidence: Ours just sets `groupIds: addToGroup(...)` on each selected element via mutateElement, then triggerUpdate+commit. Excalidraw additionally: moves all group members to be contiguous at the z-position of the highest member (`elementsBeforeGroup`/`elementsInGroup`/`elementsAfterGroup` reassembly + syncMovedIndices, lines 169-182) and updates appState via `selectGroup(newGroupId, ...)`. Ours leaves interleaved non-group elements between group members in the z-stack.
expected: Grouping pulls members together in z-order (group renders as one contiguous stack) and selects the new group.
actual: Members keep their scattered z-order; elements not in the group can remain visually sandwiched between group members; selectedGroupIds isn't set.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed at the cited locations. Excalidraw's actionGroup.tsx:166-182 explicitly reassembles z-order after assigning the new groupId: it computes `elementsInGroup` (getElementsInGroup), finds the highest member's index, then rebuilds the element array as `[...elementsBeforeGroup, ...elementsInGroup, ...elementsAfterGroup]` (filtering group members out of the "before" slice) and runs `syncMovedIndices`. This guarantees the group renders as one contiguous z-stack at the position of its topmost member. It also updates appState via `selectGroup(newGroupId, ...)` (lines 187-191).

Our port's `groupSelected()` (draw-controller.svelte.ts:978-993) only does the groupId assignment: for each selected element it calls `mutateElement(el, map, { groupIds: addToGroup(...) })`, then `triggerUpdate()` + `#commit()`. There is NO z-order reassembly. The fractional indices / array order are left untouched, so any non-group element whose z-index falls between two grouped members stays visually sandwiched between them after grouping. Since the port is a faithful Excalidraw renderer (renders elements in array/fractional-index order — confirmed src/lib/excalidraw/renderer present, and #applyHistory uses orderByFractionalIndex), this contiguity difference is genuinely user-visible: group a back element and a front element with an ungrouped element between them, and in our port the ungrouped element still paints in the middle of the group, whereas in Excalidraw the group is pulled together. All required helpers (getElementsInGroup, isElementInGroup, syncMovedIndices, selectGroup, newElementWith, getNonDeletedElements) already exist in the port, so this is achievable parity, not out-of-scope.

The secondary claim — "selectedGroupIds isn't set" — is literally true (the controller never writes appState.selectedGroupIds; it models selection as a flat selectedIds Set and expands group membership at click/marquee time via #withGroupMembers). In the port's architecture this is benign: after grouping, the same element ids stay selected and subsequent clicks select the whole group via getElementsInGroup expansion. So the selection-state divergence does not by itself produce wrong behavior. The z-order divergence is the real, user-visible bug; I'm marking it a (partial) functional divergence rather than benign because the contiguity reorder genuinely changes rendered output.

title: lockSelected does not create a temporary group for multi-selection locks (lockedMultiSelections)
evidence: Ours: for each selected element `mutateElement(el, map, { locked: true })`, then deselect. Excalidraw, when locking a multi-element selection that isn't already one group, assigns a shared temporary `newGroupId` and records it in `lockedMultiSelections` so the locked set stays a unit and unlock-all can strip that temp group (actionUnlockAllElements lines 171-188). Our unlockAll (draw-controller 957) just clears `locked` with no temp-group cleanup.
expected: Locking a loose multi-selection keeps it grouped as one locked unit (temp group recorded), so it unlocks/selects back as a unit.
actual: Each element is locked independently with no temp group; the previously-locked-together set won't re-select as a unit on unlock.

verdict.reasoning:
Both cited locations say what the claim describes. Excalidraw's actionElementLock.ts (lines 62-106) computes `isAGroup = selectedElements.length > 1 && elementsAreInSameGroup(...)`, and for a loose (non-already-grouped) multi-selection sets `newGroupId = randomId()`, adds it to every selected element's `groupIds`, and records it in `appState.lockedMultiSelections` (lines 65-73, 88-91). actionUnlockAllElements (lines 168-211) strips any temp group via `nextGroupIds.filter(gid => !appState.lockedMultiSelections[gid])`, then re-selects all unlocked elements (`selectedElementIds` from lockedElements, `selectedGroupIds` via selectGroupsFromGivenElements) and resets `lockedMultiSelections`/`activeLockedId`.

Our port (draw-controller.svelte.ts:942-954) does `for (const el of sel) mutateElement(el, map, { locked: true })` then `#select(null)` — no temp group, no lockedMultiSelections entry, no activeLockedId. unlockAll (957-970) just flips `locked:false` per element with no group cleanup and crucially NO re-selection. The parallel commands.svelte.ts (toggleLockSelection/unlockAll, lines 357-374) is identical in behavior.

The lockedMultiSelections and activeLockedId fields DO exist in our port's appState/types/delta/store, and the interactiveScene renderer (line 1691) has a branch that highlights the locked unit when activeLockedId is set — but grep confirms activeLockedId is NEVER set to a non-null value anywhere in the port, so that branch is dead. The group helpers (elementsAreInSameGroup, getElementsInGroup, addToGroup, selectGroupsFromGivenElements) all exist, so the feature is implementable but simply unwired in the lock path.

Real user-visible divergences: (1) after Unlock-all-elements, Excalidraw selects all the just-unlocked elements; our port leaves selection empty. (2) The entire 'click a locked element on canvas → see the whole locked unit highlighted (dashed box via activeLockedId) → unlock it as one unit' interaction is absent in our port. These manifest for the realistic action of locking a multi-selection then unlocking. The claim's exact phrasing 'won't re-select as a unit on unlock' is slightly imprecise (Excalidraw strips the temp group during unlock, so it re-selects as individual elements, not a persistent group), but the underlying mechanism and the existence of a behavioral difference are correct.
